title: The Lifecycle of a Bug
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: status
title: The Lifecycle of a Bug
tracker: Jira-compatible
issue: PAY-4821
summary: Checkout total ignores promo code on second apply
---
A single defect, issue PAY-4821, walks the board from the moment a user files it
to the moment the fix ships. Each plane is one status column. The x offset moves
the card left to right across the board so the progression reads like a Kanban
swimlane; the y offset nudges side-paths (reopened, won't fix) off the main row.

Start at [[z=0|New]] and follow the happy path to [[z=5|Closed]].

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="New"
# New

A user reported PAY-4821 through the in-app feedback widget at 09:14.

**What we know**

- Applying a promo code twice doubles the discount instead of rejecting the
  second apply.
- Reported on web checkout, Chrome 124, guest cart.
- Severity unset, no owner, no reproduction confirmed.

**Entry criteria**

- The report has a title and a description.
- It is not an obvious duplicate of an open issue.

A triager picks this up next. If it cannot be reproduced it bounces back here;
otherwise it advances to [[z=1|Triaged]].

@plane z=1 x=1 y=0 label="Triaged"
# Triaged

Reproduced on staging in two attempts. Now it is a real, prioritized bug.

**Assessment**

- Severity: **High** (revenue impact, customer can underpay).
- Priority: P1, target this sprint.
- Component: `checkout/promo-engine`.
- Assigned to: the Payments squad.

**Reproduction (confirmed)**

1. Add any item to the cart.
2. Apply promo `SAVE10`. Total drops 10 percent. Correct.
3. Apply `SAVE10` a second time. Total drops another 10 percent. Wrong.

With a confirmed repro and an owner, the card moves to [[z=2|In Progress]].
If priority gets bumped down below the cut line it can land in [[z=4.5|Won't Fix]].

@plane z=2 x=2 y=0 label="In Progress"
# In Progress

An engineer has the branch `fix/PAY-4821-double-promo` open and is writing code.

**Root cause**

The promo engine keys applied discounts by `code` but never checks whether that
code is already present in `appliedPromos` before pushing a new entry. The guard
exists for gift cards and was simply never added for percentage promos.

```swift
guard !appliedPromos.contains(where: { $0.code == promo.code }) else {
    throw PromoError.alreadyApplied(promo.code)
}
appliedPromos.append(promo)
```

**Plan**

- Add the duplicate guard above.
- Add a regression test for the double-apply path.

When the branch compiles and the test is written, it goes up for [[z=3|In Review]].

@plane z=3 x=3 y=0 label="In Review"
# In Review

Pull request #1190 is open and waiting on a second pair of eyes.

**Checklist**

- [x] Duplicate guard added to `PromoEngine.apply`.
- [x] Regression test `testDoublePromoRejected` passes.
- [x] CI green on macOS and Linux.
- [ ] One approving review.

**Reviewer note (open)**

> Good catch on the guard. Can we also surface a friendly message in the UI
> instead of a raw error toast? Not a blocker, file a follow-up.

A follow-up ticket PAY-4830 was filed for the UI copy. Once approved and merged,
the fix is deployed and the card lands in [[z=4|Resolved]]. If review uncovers a
deeper problem, it drops back to [[z=2|In Progress]].

@plane z=4 x=4 y=0 label="Resolved"
# Resolved

Merged to `main` and deployed to production at 16:42. The engineer marks the bug
fixed and hands it to QA for verification.

**Deployed change**

- Second apply of any promo code now returns a 409 with `alreadyApplied`.
- The cart total reflects exactly one discount per unique code.

**Awaiting verification**

QA will re-run the original reproduction steps against production. Two outcomes:

- Verified fixed, advance to [[z=5|Closed]].
- Still broken, send it to [[z=2.5|Reopened]].

@plane z=2.5 x=2 y=-1 label="Reopened"
# Reopened

QA found that the fix works for `SAVE10` but stacking two *different* codes
(`SAVE10` then `FREESHIP`) still double-discounts. The original guard only
matched identical codes.

**Why it came back**

The duplicate check compared `code` equality, but the real rule is "at most one
percentage promo at a time," which is a category check, not a string check.

This card returns to [[z=2|In Progress]] with the sharpened requirement. The
loop from build to review to verify runs again until QA signs off.

@plane z=4.5 x=4 y=-1 label="Won't Fix"
# Won't Fix

Not every bug ships a fix. This terminal state records a deliberate decision.

**When a card lands here**

- The behavior is working as intended once requirements are clarified.
- The cost to fix outweighs the impact (rare edge, tiny audience).
- It is a duplicate of another tracked issue.

For PAY-4821 this lane stays empty: the revenue impact made the fix mandatory.
It is shown here to complete the board. A resolved-as-wontfix card is closed with
a rationale and is not reopened without new evidence.

@plane z=5 x=5 y=0 label="Closed"
# Closed

QA verified the broadened fix against production. PAY-4821 is closed.

**Final state**

- Status: Closed, resolution Fixed.
- Cycle time: 3 days, 7 hours from [[z=0|New]] to here.
- Follow-up PAY-4830 (friendly UI message) remains open in its own lane.

**Lessons captured**

- Model business rules as categories, not string matches, when "only one of a
  kind is allowed."
- The first fix passed its test but not the real-world case; the regression
  suite gained a cross-code stacking test so [[z=2.5|Reopened]] does not recur.

The card rests here. A closed bug is the quiet end of the line.

title: Chef Service Rush - Tickets to Pass
src:
---
3md: 1.0

title: Chef Service Rush - Tickets to Pass
view: present

---

Dinner service as a timed stack. Each plane shows the kitchen at a different
minute, from first tickets to the clean pass.

@plane z=0 label="5:57 Prep"
# 5:57 PM - Prep Locked

```
STATION MAP

[ grill ] steaks rested     [ saute ] pans hot
[ fry   ] baskets staged    [ pass  ] empty rail

ticket rail: 0
```

- Mise checked
- Sauces hot
- Expo calls: "Doors in three"

@plane z=1 label="6:04 First Wave"
# 6:04 PM - First Wave

```
TICKET RAIL

  12  14  18
 [2] [4] [3] covers

GRILL: 2 steaks
SAUTE: 3 pastas
FRY:   4 fries
PASS:  garnish ready
```

The rush begins clean. The key is keeping proteins and pastas aligned to the
same pickup window.

@plane z=2 label="6:17 Peak"
# 6:17 PM - Peak Heat

```
TICKET RAIL

  21  23  25  28  31
 [5] [2] [6] [4] [3]

GRILL: ████████ 8 items
SAUTE: ██████   6 pans
FRY:   █████    5 baskets
PASS:  3 plates waiting
```

Expo splits ticket 25: mains fire now, sides hold ninety seconds. The rail is
busy but still readable.

@plane z=3 label="6:29 Recovery"
# 6:29 PM - Recovery Window

```
SENT: 21, 23, 25
HELD: 28 sauce
NEW:  34 dessert

PASS
┌────────────┬────────────┐
│ steak x2   │ pasta x1   │
│ fries x3   │ salad x2   │
└────────────┴────────────┘
```

One sauce delay threatens the rhythm. Expo moves garnish forward and keeps the
next tickets from stacking behind it.

@plane z=4 label="6:41 Clear Rail"
# 6:41 PM - Rail Clear

```
TICKET RAIL

 [ clear ] [ clear ] [ clear ]

GRILL: wipe down
SAUTE: refill pasta water
FRY:   skim oil
PASS:  reset herbs
```

The rush resolves. The best service map shows pressure without losing sequence.

title: Release Pipeline (CI/CD DAG)
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: job
title: Release Pipeline (CI/CD DAG)
category: Engineering
schema: pipeline/1
machine: corvid-ci
roots: ["0", "1"]
sink: "8"
legend: o=●
description: A CI/CD pipeline as a directed acyclic graph. Each plane is a job; each [[z=N|job]] link is a "needs" (dependency) edge pointing upstream. Parse planes as nodes and links as edges, then topologically sort to get a valid run order.
---
A release pipeline encoded as a DAG. Read each job as a card, or follow the
[[z=2|needs]] links upstream to recover the dependency graph. Jobs with no
needs (lint, unit-test) are the roots; notify is the sink. Fan-out at build,
fan-in at deploy-prod.

@plane z=0 label="lint" runs_on=ubuntu needs="" duration="40s"
# lint

Static analysis and formatting gate. No upstream dependencies; runs immediately.

- needs: nothing (root job)

@plane z=1 label="unit-test" runs_on=ubuntu needs="" duration="3m"
# unit-test

Fast unit suite. Also a root; runs in parallel with [[z=0|lint]].

- needs: nothing (root job)

@plane z=2 label="build" runs_on=ubuntu needs="0,1" duration="5m"
# build

Compile and bundle the artifact. Fan-in: waits for both gates.

- needs -> [[z=0|lint]]
- needs -> [[z=1|unit-test]]

@plane z=3 label="deploy-staging" runs_on=deploy needs="2" duration="2m"
# deploy-staging

Push the built artifact to the staging environment.

- needs -> [[z=2|build]]

@plane z=4 label="e2e" runs_on=ubuntu needs="3" duration="8m"
# e2e

End-to-end browser tests against staging.

- needs -> [[z=3|deploy-staging]]

@plane z=5 label="security-scan" runs_on=ubuntu needs="2" duration="6m"
# security-scan

SAST + dependency audit on the build. Runs in parallel with the staging branch.

- needs -> [[z=2|build]]

@plane z=6 label="deploy-prod" runs_on=deploy needs="4,5" duration="3m"
# deploy-prod

Promote to production. Fan-in: gated on both e2e and the security scan.

- needs -> [[z=4|e2e]]
- needs -> [[z=5|security-scan]]

@plane z=7 label="smoke-test" runs_on=ubuntu needs="6" duration="1m"
# smoke-test

Post-deploy smoke checks against production.

- needs -> [[z=6|deploy-prod]]

@plane z=8 label="notify" runs_on=ubuntu needs="7" duration="10s"
# notify

Announce the release to the team channel. Sink job.

- needs -> [[z=7|smoke-test]]

title: Color Grading Layers - Night Market Still
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: layer
title: Color Grading Layers - Night Market Still
view: stack
category: Creative
---

A color grade broken into visible layers. Each plane adds one adjustment so the
final look has a trail of decisions behind it.

@plane z=0 label="Base Plate"
# Base Plate

```
┌──────────────────────────┐
│  NIGHT MARKET FRAME 017  │
│                          │
│  sign glow: neutral      │
│  shadows: flat gray      │
│  skin: slightly cool     │
│                          │
│  [ ungraded source ]     │
└──────────────────────────┘
```

- Exposure: balanced
- White point: 5600K
- Problem: the subject blends into the awnings

@plane z=1 label="Contrast Curve"
# Contrast Curve

```
Luma curve

100 |                 *
 75 |            *
 50 |       *
 25 |    *
  0 |_*________________
      toe  mids  shoulder
```

- Lift blacks just above crush
- Add a soft shoulder to protect neon signs
- Subject now separates from the background

@plane z=2 label="Neon Split"

title: Dataset Card - orders (e-commerce fact table)
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: field
title: Dataset Card - orders (e-commerce fact table)
category: Data
dataset: analytics.orders
grain: one row per placed order
schema: datacard/1
owner: data-platform
rows: "~42M"
---
A data dictionary you can read like a doc and parse like a schema. Each @plane
is one column: the label is the field name, the attrs carry the machine schema
(type, nullable, unit, range, key), and the body is what a human actually needs
(meaning, example values, and the gotcha that will bite you).

Walk it as a reader (single), or see the whole table as a board (map).

@plane z=0 label="order_id" type="uuid" nullable="false" key="primary" pii="false"
# order_id

Surrogate primary key for the order. Stable for the life of the order.

- example: `7f3a9c20-1e6b-4f8a-9b21-0c5d6e7a1234`
- gotcha: not sequential and not time-ordered. Do not sort by it to get
  chronological order. Use [[z=5|created_at]] instead.

@plane z=1 label="customer_id" type="uuid" nullable="false" key="foreign" ref="dim_customer.customer_id" pii="false"
# customer_id

Foreign key to the customer who placed the order.

- example: `c1a2b3d4-...`
- gotcha: guest checkouts get a synthetic customer_id that is unique per order,
  so counting distinct customer_id over-counts true customers by ~6 percent.

@plane z=2 label="order_status" type="enum" nullable="false" values="placed|paid|shipped|delivered|cancelled|refunded"
# order_status

Lifecycle state at the time the row was last updated.

- example: `delivered`
- gotcha: `cancelled` and `refunded` rows still carry a non-zero
  [[z=3|total_amount]]. Filter them out before summing revenue.

@plane z=3 label="total_amount" type="integer" nullable="false" unit="USD cents" range="0..100000000"
# total_amount

Order total INCLUDING tax and shipping, AFTER discounts.

- example: `4999` means $49.99
- gotcha: stored in integer cents, not dollars. Divide by 100. Mixing this with
  a dollars column is the single most common revenue bug here.

@plane z=4 label="currency" type="char" length="3" nullable="false" default="USD"
# currency

ISO 4217 currency code for [[z=3|total_amount]].

- example: `USD`, `EUR`, `JPY`
- gotcha: JPY has no minor unit, so for JPY rows `total_amount` is whole yen, not
  cents. Currency-aware conversion is required before cross-currency sums.

@plane z=5 label="created_at" type="timestamptz" nullable="false"
# created_at

When the order was placed. The canonical event time for this table.

- example: `2026-06-24T18:30:05Z`
- gotcha: stored in UTC. The storefront reports in store-local time, so daily
  counts will disagree with the app dashboard near midnight unless you convert.

@plane z=6 label="shipped_at" type="timestamptz" nullable="true"
# shipped_at

When the order shipped. NULL until it does.

- example: `2026-06-25T09:12:00Z` or `NULL`
- gotcha: NULL means "not shipped yet", not "shipped at an unknown time".
  `shipped_at - [[z=5|created_at]]` is your fulfillment latency, but only for
  non-null rows.

@plane z=7 label="item_count" type="integer" nullable="false" range="1..500"
# item_count

Number of line items on the order.

- example: `3`
- gotcha: this is distinct SKUs, not total quantity. A cart of 10 of one SKU has
  item_count = 1.

@plane z=8 label="discount_pct" type="numeric" precision="5,2" nullable="true" unit="percent" range="0..100"
# discount_pct

Order-level discount applied, as a percentage.

- example: `15.00`
- gotcha: NULL means no discount, which is NOT the same as `0.00` (an explicitly
  recorded zero-value promo). Coalesce to 0 only if you do not care about that
  distinction.

| E | Tomb Guardian |
| D | Sealed vault door (requires dungeon key) |
| $ | **Dungeon Key** + 120gp |

**Alder's status:** ❤️❤️   🪄 1 charge   🗡️ Dagger +1   🗝️ Dungeon Key

> *Sixteen skeletal adventurers line the hall on their pedestals, arms crossed, eye sockets dark. The last one on the right is holding something.*
>
> *It's a map. **It's a map of this dungeon.** You were never supposed to make it this far.*

**The Tomb Guardians are immune to the Scroll of Light.** Save that charge.

[[z=4|Face the Dragon]]

@plane z=4 label="Floor 5 — The Dragon's Hoard"

```
##########
#........#
#..OOOOO.#
#.O$$$O..#
#.O$*$O..#
#.O$$$O..#
#..OOOOO.#
#........#
#...E....#
####@####
```

**Floor 5 — The Dragon's Hoard**

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| O | Gilded column |
| $ | Treasure pile |
| * | **Vyndrath** — Ancient Wyrm (Boss) |
| E | Dragon Cultist (minion) |
| @ | You arrive here, alone, with 1 spell charge |

**Alder's final status:** ❤️❤️   🪄 1 charge   🗡️ Dagger +1   🗝️ Dungeon Key *(no door down here)*   💰 172gp

---

### Boss: Vyndrath the Moss-Drake

> *He is the size of a carriage and smells like a hot kettle. One eye opens. He says, in perfect common tongue:*
>
> *"Another one. You people are relentless."*

**Victory conditions (pick one):**

- [ ] Defeat Vyndrath (spend spell charge + 1 ❤️, roll well)
- [ ] Negotiate — offer the **spore pouch** (he's been sick; he just wants to rest)
- [ ] Steal the hoard key and leave without a fight *(you coward; you brilliant coward)*

**Reward:** 🏆 **850gp** · 📖 *Tome of Returning* · The stairs go **up**.

> *The impractical boots, it turns out, were perfectly suited for running.*

title: Dungeon Run #4471
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: depth
title: Dungeon Run #4471
view: elevator
seed: 4471
character: Witch (Hexblade spec)
started: 2026-06-23T02:14
run_result: DEAD on floor 9
category: Games
---
> *You descend. Each floor a new roll of the dice. Scroll down to fall deeper.*

@plane z=0 label="Floor 1 — Vestibule"
# Floor 1 — Vestibule
**HP: 20/20** | Gold: 0 | Depth: 1

You wake on cold stone. Torchlight stutters.

**Room: Empty corridor.** Dust. Old blood, not yours.

You find a **Rusted Dagger** +1 in a collapsed chest.

- [x] Picked up Rusted Dagger
- [ ] Found the staircase

> *The walls breathe here. They always do on Floor 1.*

@plane z=1 label="Floor 2 — Rat Warren"
# Floor 2 — Rat Warren
**HP: 17/20** | Gold: 4 | Depth: 2

**Room: Nest chamber.** The rats were enormous. Three of them.

You took **3 damage** — claws through the boot leather. Traded blows with the alpha until it stopped moving.

Looted: `4 gold`, a `Torch x2`, and something wet you decide not to examine.

**New passive unlocked:** *Vermin Sense* — you now hear rats through walls. Useful and unsettling.

> *Smells like rot and old fur. You are getting good at ignoring smells.*

@plane z=2 label="Floor 3 — Merchant"
# Floor 3 — Merchant
**HP: 17/20** | Gold: 4 | Depth: 3

**Room: Wandering Merchant — Grell**

Grell has three fingers and a cart that shouldn't fit in this dungeon. He doesn't explain.

| Item | Cost |
|---|---|
| Health Potion | 6 gold |
| Hex Scroll | 3 gold |
| Iron Helm +2 DEF | 10 gold |

You buy the **Hex Scroll** for 3 gold. You are now broke.

Grell winks. "Come back if you survive floor 5."

> *You will not see Grell again. This is always true.*

@plane z=3 label="Floor 4 — Trap Hall"
# Floor 4 — Trap Hall
**HP: 12/20** | Gold: 1 | Depth: 4

**Room: Pressure-plate corridor.** You spotted two. Missed the third.

Dart trap: **5 damage.** The poison kind. You used the Hex Scroll to purge it — counterintuitively effective.

Found a **secret door** (Vermin Sense triggered — rats avoid the east wall). Behind it: `1 gold coin` and a dead adventurer with a **journal, page 47 only.**

> *"Floor 9 is wrong," the dead adventurer wrote. "The geometry doesn't add up."*

@plane z=4 label="Floor 5 — Boss: Silt Golem"
# Floor 5 — Boss: Silt Golem
**HP: 8/20** | Gold: 1 | Depth: 5

**Boss Room: Silt Golem, CR 4**

Three rounds. Brutal.

- **Round 1:** You hexbind its left arm. It slows. You don't.
- **Round 2:** It regenerates. You use the last torch as a weapon. This works exactly once.
- **Round 3:** Desperate lunge with the Rusted Dagger into the clay throat-seam. It crumbles.

**Loot:** `Golem Core` (passive: +2 DEF when below 50% HP), `11 gold`, `Health Potion`.

You drink the potion immediately. **HP: 16/20.**

> *Your hands are full of grey silt. It takes days to wash out. You will not have days.*

See [[z=7|the floor that breaks you]] if you want to skip ahead.

@plane z=5 label="Floor 6 — Rest Shrine"
# Floor 6 — Rest Shrine
**HP: 16/20** | Gold: 12 | Depth: 6

**Room: Shrine of the Hollow Saint**

First rest since Floor 1. You sit. The shrine hums.

**Choices (pick one):**
- [x] **Heal to full** (HP: 20/20)
- [ ] Level up Hexbind (longer duration)
- [ ] Reveal Floor 7 map

You heal. The practical choice. The boring choice. The alive choice.

You find graffiti carved into the altar stone: *"Leif was here. Floor 8. Good luck."*

Someone with your name made it further than you expected.

@plane z=6 label="Floor 7 — Flooded"
# Floor 7 — Flooded
**HP: 20/20** | Gold: 12 | Depth: 7

**Room: Flooded Tomb.** Knee-deep black water.

Movement costs double. The **Golem Core** activates — you're at full HP so the +2 DEF does nothing.

Two **Drowned Knights** rise. Slow, relentless, silent. You hex one, kite the other, use the pillars.

**6 damage total.** HP: 14/20.

Treasure chest is underwater. You hold your breath and retrieve: `Depth Chart` (reveals stairs on each floor) and a `Silver Ring` (unknown enchantment).

> *The water was warm. You try not to think about why.*

@plane z=7 label="Floor 8 — The Collector"
# Floor 8 — The Collector
**HP: 14/20** | Gold: 12 | Depth: 8

**Room: The Collector's gallery.**

She has been down here longer than the dungeon has existed. She collects *mistakes*. She offers a trade.

> "Your Rusted Dagger — the one you've carried since Floor 1 — for my **Blade of Unmaking**."

**Blade of Unmaking** stats: `+5 ATK`, `on-kill: echo the kill once`

You hesitate. The dagger has killed seventeen things. It has *weight*.

**You trade it.** The dagger disappears into a glass case labeled *"Attachment."*

> *You immediately regret this. You will use it well anyway.*

@plane z=8 label="Floor 9 — DEAD"
# Floor 9 — The Wrong Room
**HP: 0/20** | Gold: 12 | Depth: 9

**Room: The geometry doesn't add up.**

The dead adventurer was right. Floor 9 folds back on itself. The map is useless. You've been in this room before — you're sure of it — but the door behind you leads somewhere new each time.

The **Lich Archivist** was waiting. Not spawned. *Waiting.* Like it knew your seed.

You used the Blade of Unmaking. The echo-kill proc'd. It wasn't enough.

**14 damage. Dead.**

---

**Run complete.**
- Floors cleared: 8
- Kills: 23
- Gold unspent: 12 *(classic)*
- Items: Blade of Unmaking, Silver Ring (still unknown), Depth Chart, Iron-nil helm you never bought

**Unlock:** Hexblade Ascendant (available next run)

> *The dungeon resets. Seed 4472 is already generating.*
> *You will be back in four minutes.*

title: The Hollow Below
src:
---
3md: 1.0
category: Games
axis: room
title: The Hollow Below
subtitle: a dungeon you play by clicking
view: single
start: "0"
win: "6"
---
A dungeon crawl with no engine: every exit is a cross-plane link you click to
move. Find the key, avoid the dark, reach the vault. Begin at [[z=0|the mouth of the cave]].

@plane z=0 label="The Cave Mouth"
# The Cave Mouth

Cold air breathes up out of the dark. Your torch gutters. Two ways lead down.

- Take the wide [[z=1|north corridor]]
- Squeeze into the narrow [[z=2|side passage]]

@plane z=1 label="North Corridor"
# North Corridor

Dripping stone. Bones, picked clean, in a corner. Ahead: a great **iron door**,
its lock shaped like a snarling mouth. To the left, stairs spiral down.

- Try the [[z=3|iron door]]
- Descend the [[z=4|spiral stairs]]
- Back to the [[z=2|side passage]]

@plane z=2 label="Side Passage"
# Side Passage

Tight enough to scrape your shoulders. Half-buried in grit, something glints:
a **rusty iron key**. You pocket it.

- Return to the [[z=1|north corridor]]
- Press on to the [[z=5|old crypt]]

@plane z=3 label="The Iron Door"
# The Iron Door

The snarling lock waits. If you found the rusty key in the side passage, it
turns with a scream of metal and the door swings wide.

- I have the key: enter the [[z=6|treasure vault]]
- No key? Go find it in the [[z=2|side passage]]

@plane z=4 label="Spiral Stairs"
# Spiral Stairs

Down and down. The air turns wet. The steps end at a black underground river,
and a half-rotted raft knocks against the bank.

- Take the [[z=8|underground river]]
- Climb back to the [[z=1|north corridor]]

@plane z=5 label="The Old Crypt"
# The Old Crypt

Stone coffins, lids ajar. Something in the dark is breathing. A low arch leads
on; the largest tomb tempts you to look inside.

- Slip quietly past to the [[z=6|treasure vault]]
- Pry open the [[z=7|great tomb]]

@plane z=6 label="Treasure Vault  (WIN)"
# The Treasure Vault

Gold drowns the torchlight. A crown, a hundred coins, a sword that hums. You
fill your pack and walk out richer than kings.

**You win.**

- Play again from the [[z=0|cave mouth]]

@plane z=7 label="The Dark  (DEATH)"
# The Dark Takes You

Something vast uncoils. The torch goes out. The last thing you hear is your own
heartbeat, and then not even that.

**You died.**

- Try again from the [[z=0|cave mouth]]

@plane z=8 label="Underground River"
# Underground River

The raft holds. Two currents split the black water: one drifts toward a faint
glow, the other into total dark.

- Steer toward the glow and the [[z=6|treasure vault]]
- Let the current pull you into the [[z=7|dark]]

title: The Periodic Table by Period
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: period
title: The Periodic Table by Period
subject: Chemistry
source: IUPAC standard atomic data
---
The periodic table organized by period (row). Each plane is one period, stacked
along the period axis. Within a period, atomic number increases left to right and
a new electron shell fills. For the heavy synthetic elements see
[[z=7|period 7]].

@plane z=1 label="Period 1"
# Period 1

Only two elements. The 1s subshell fills.

| Z | Symbol | Name     | Category   |
|---|--------|----------|------------|
| 1 | H      | Hydrogen | nonmetal   |
| 2 | He     | Helium   | noble gas  |

> Hydrogen sits above the alkali metals by convention but behaves like neither
> group cleanly. Helium completes the n=1 shell with just two electrons.

@plane z=2 label="Period 2"
# Period 2

The 2s and 2p subshells fill. This row contains the backbone elements of life.

| Z  | Symbol | Name      | Category          |
|----|--------|-----------|-------------------|
| 3  | Li     | Lithium   | alkali metal      |
| 4  | Be     | Beryllium | alkaline earth    |
| 5  | B      | Boron     | metalloid         |
| 6  | C      | Carbon    | nonmetal          |
| 7  | N      | Nitrogen  | nonmetal          |
| 8  | O      | Oxygen    | nonmetal          |
| 9  | F      | Fluorine  | halogen           |
| 10 | Ne     | Neon      | noble gas         |

@plane z=3 label="Period 3"
# Period 3

The 3s and 3p subshells fill. Includes the most abundant element in Earth's
crust by mass after oxygen.

| Z  | Symbol | Name       | Category        |
|----|--------|------------|-----------------|
| 11 | Na     | Sodium     | alkali metal    |
| 12 | Mg     | Magnesium  | alkaline earth  |
| 13 | Al     | Aluminium  | post-transition |
| 14 | Si     | Silicon    | metalloid       |
| 15 | P      | Phosphorus | nonmetal        |
| 16 | S      | Sulfur     | nonmetal        |
| 17 | Cl     | Chlorine   | halogen         |
| 18 | Ar     | Argon      | noble gas       |

@plane z=4 label="Period 4"
# Period 4

The first period with the d-block. The 4s fills, then the 3d transition metals,
then the 4p.

## s-block and p-block
- K (19) potassium, Ca (20) calcium
- Ga (31) through Kr (36) finish the row

## First transition series (3d)
Sc (21), Ti (22), V (23), Cr (24), Mn (25), Fe (26), Co (27), Ni (28),
Cu (29), Zn (30).

> Iron (Fe, 26) is the most stable nucleus per nucleon, the endpoint of stellar
> fusion in massive stars.

@plane z=7 label="Period 7"
# Period 7

The heaviest known elements, almost all synthetic and radioactive. The 7s, 5f
actinides, 6d, and 7p fill across the row.

- Begins with Fr (87) francium and Ra (88) radium.
- The actinides span Ac (89) through Lr (103), including U (92) uranium and
  Pu (94) plutonium.
- The superheavy elements run through Og (118) oganesson, the highest atomic
  number confirmed by IUPAC.

> Most period 7 elements past uranium do not occur naturally in meaningful
> amounts and were first made in particle accelerators or reactors.

title: Major Events in the Evolution of Life
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: time
title: Major Events in the Evolution of Life
author: CorvidLabs Gallery
span: ~3.8 billion years
---
A walk along deep time. Each plane is one milestone on the Z axis, ordered by
how long ago it happened (z is millions of years before present, so larger z is
further back). Read from the deepest plane forward, or jump straight to
[[z=0.3|the rise of humans]].

@plane z=3800 x=0 y=0 label="First Life (~3.8 Ga)"
# First Life

The oldest chemical and fossil traces of life appear in rocks roughly 3.5 to 3.8
billion years old. The earliest cells were single, membrane-bound prokaryotes
with no nucleus, living in the oceans.

- **Form:** simple anaerobic cells (no oxygen required)
- **Energy:** chemosynthesis near hydrothermal vents and shallow seas
- **Legacy:** the universal genetic code, shared by every organism since

Everything that follows, from a redwood to a blue whale, descends from this
single-celled starting point. The next great leap was learning to eat sunlight:
see [[z=2400|the Great Oxidation]].

@plane z=2400 x=2 y=0 label="Great Oxidation (~2.4 Ga)"
# The Great Oxidation Event

Cyanobacteria evolved oxygen-producing photosynthesis and, over hundreds of
millions of years, flooded the atmosphere with free oxygen. For most existing
microbes this was a poison, triggering one of Earth's earliest mass extinctions.

| Before | After |
| --- | --- |
| Trace atmospheric oxygen | Oxygen-rich atmosphere |
| Anaerobic life dominant | Aerobic respiration possible |

Oxygen was the fuel that later powered large, active animals. The first complex
cells came next: continue to [[z=1800|the eukaryotic cell]].

@plane z=1800 x=4 y=0 label="Eukaryotes (~1.8 Ga)"
# The Eukaryotic Cell

A larger, more complex cell type arose, defined by a nucleus and internal
organelles. The leading explanation is **endosymbiosis**: one cell engulfed
another, and the captive became a permanent partner.

1. An ancestral host cell absorbed an oxygen-using bacterium.
2. The partner became the **mitochondrion**, the cell's power plant.
3. A separate event gave plant ancestors the **chloroplast**.

This partnership is why your cells still carry mitochondrial DNA distinct from
the DNA in your nucleus. With efficient energy in hand, life eventually went
multicellular and big: jump to [[z=540|the Cambrian Explosion]].

@plane z=540 x=6 y=0 label="Cambrian Explosion (~540 Ma)"
# The Cambrian Explosion

In a geologically brief window, most major animal body plans appeared in the
fossil record. Hard shells, jointed limbs, compound eyes, and active predators
all show up together, preserved beautifully in sites like the Burgess Shale.

- First arthropods, mollusks, and early chordates (our distant relatives)
- The arms race between predator and prey drove rapid innovation
- Vision and mineralized skeletons became widespread

Life had been microscopic for over three billion years; in the Cambrian it got
complicated fast. From these chordates came backbones, and eventually limbs for
land: move forward to [[z=375|life moves ashore]].

@plane z=375 x=8 y=0 label="Life on Land (~375 Ma)"
# Vertebrates Move Ashore

Lobe-finned fishes such as *Tiktaalik* show the transition from water to land:
sturdy, weight-bearing fins, a flexible neck, and lungs alongside gills. Their
descendants, the tetrapods, gave rise to amphibians, reptiles, birds, and
mammals.

> Tiktaalik is often called a "fishapod," part fish and part four-legged animal,
> a clear snapshot of an evolutionary transition in progress.

Plants and arthropods had greened the land first, building the food web these
pioneers walked into. The last stop on this timeline is the youngest branch:
arrive at [[z=0.3|the human lineage]].

@plane z=0.3 x=10 y=0 label="Humans (~300 Ka)"
# The Human Lineage

Anatomically modern *Homo sapiens* appears in Africa around 300,000 years ago,
the latest twig on an ancient tree. On the scale of this document, all of
recorded human history is a thin sliver at the very surface.

- **Brain and language:** complex cognition, symbolic thought, culture
- **Tools and fire:** technology that reshaped diet and environment
- **Migration:** out of Africa and across every continent

Stand here at the present and look back: every step, from [[z=3800|the first
cell]] through oxygen, complex cells, and limbs on land, was a prerequisite for
this plane existing at all.

title: Fallingwater: Floor by Floor
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: floor
title: Fallingwater: Floor by Floor
view: elevator
architect: Frank Lloyd Wright
completed: 1939
client: Edgar J. Kaufmann Sr.
site: Bear Run, Mill Run, Pennsylvania
style: Organic Architecture
structure: Reinforced concrete cantilevers over waterfall
category: Information
---
Fallingwater, Bear Run PA — ride the axis from bedrock to roof.
Each floor is a lesson in how Wright stacked compression and release.

@plane z=0 label="Bedrock & Stream"

# z=0 — The Ground Plane

The building doesn't sit *on* the waterfall. It sits *over* it.

Wright anchored the structure to a single sandstone ledge at elevation **+0.0 ft**, letting Bear Run flow uninterrupted beneath. The original boulder that the Kaufmanns used as a diving board still protrudes through the living room floor — Wright refused to blast it.

**Key moves:**
- Stone piers carry all vertical load to bedrock
- Floor slab at stream level is exposed aggregate, continuous with the site
- The sound of the falls audible from every room above
- Approach path from the east kept low and compressed to amplify the reveal

> "The visit to the waterfall in the woods stays with you…"
> — Edgar Kaufmann Jr.

**Structural note:** Three 15-ft cantilevered trays extend west, each balanced by the stone cores behind them. The concrete was under-reinforced at construction; major restoration completed **1997–2002** added post-tensioned rods.

@plane z=1 label="Living Level"

# z=1 — Living Floor (+12 ft)

The **main living room** is the payoff. After a compressed entry stair, the ceiling snaps to **6'4"** above the fireplace and opens to **14'0"** at the glass corners.

**Plan geometry:**
- 2,400 sq ft total floor plate, 1,500 sq ft living area
- Four corner glass panes meet with no mullion at the corner — a 1939 miracle of detailing
- Hatch covers in the floor open to let a ladder down to the stream pool below
- Built-in seating, bookshelves, and a globe bar make the room self-contained

**Materials palette:**
| Surface | Material |
|---|---|
| Floor | Waxed flagstone (local sandstone) |
| Walls | Rough-cut sandstone, coursed to match site strata |
| Ceilings | Ochre-painted concrete soffit |
| Glazing | Original single-pane, Cherokee red steel frames |

[[z=2|Up to the guest terrace →]]

**Circulation:** The stair to the stream descends from the living room hatch — a gesture of total integration between inside and outside.

@plane z=2 label="First Terrace"

# z=2 — Kaufmann Bedroom & Terrace A (+22 ft)

The master suite occupies the southwest corner, private but never separate. The bedroom ceiling drops to **6'8"** — low enough to feel sheltered, high enough to avoid oppression.

**Terrace A** is the first of the three great horizontal planes:
- 850 sq ft of reinforced concrete deck
- No railing on the west edge — just a 2" raised curb
- Oriented toward the afternoon sun and the rhododendron hillside
- The tray reads from below as pure sky-plane, hovering over the living room terrace

**Wright's section logic:**
Each tray shifts slightly east or west of the one below, creating a pinwheel composition. No two floors stack directly — every bedroom looks over the roof of the room below.

```
Plan offset diagram (schematic):
  [TERRACE C]  ←shifted east
     [TERRACE B]  ←shifted west
  [TERRACE A]  ←shifted east
[LIVING ROOM]  ←ground anchor
```

The stone fireplace core, shared with the floor below, is the hinge that makes the pinwheeling possible.

@plane z=3 label="Bridge & Guest Wing"

# z=3 — Covered Bridge & Guest House (+32 ft)

A **136-ft trellis bridge** connects the main house to the guest suite above the hillside. It is simultaneously:

- A covered walkway (roof, no walls)
- A retaining wall for the hillside garden
- A datum line that anchors the horizontal composition from across the run

**Guest suite facts:**
- 600 sq ft, one bedroom, private plunge pool carved into the rock
- Completed **1939**, same year as main house
- Staff quarters below the bridge at grade
- Servant stair concealed in the stone retaining pier

The bridge is where Wright's section becomes undeniable: you walk *through the hill* to reach the house, then step out onto the bridge and the entire composition — three horizontal trays, waterfall, rhododendron, sky — reads as a single simultaneous moment.

> Wright called the horizontal planes "the democracy of the open hand."
> The verticals are the stone — ancient, fixed, local.

[[z=4|Up to the roof level →]]

@plane z=4 label="Second Terrace"

# z=4 — Second Bedroom Level & Terrace B (+32 ft)

Two secondary bedrooms occupy this level, each with direct access to **Terrace B**, the largest of the three decks at **1,100 sq ft**.

**Spatial lessons:**
- Ceiling height drops further to **6'4"** — the compression intensifies as you rise
- Built-in beds, dressers, and window seats eliminate the need for freestanding furniture
- Each window is a framed view: Wright chose tree canopy, never sky

**Terrace B details:**
- The east edge terminates in a curved concrete planter — no right angles at the perimeter
- Cherokee red paint on all steel; the color was mixed to match autumn foliage
- A single curved step transitions between terrace levels — 4" rise, 24" tread

**Acoustic condition:** Terrace B is directly above the waterfall. On wet years, spray reaches the underside of the slab. The roar of Bear Run is loudest here — a deliberate choice. Wright wanted the falls heard, not merely seen.

@plane z=5 label="Roof Garden"

# z=5 — Roof Terrace C & Loggia (+48 ft)

The **topmost plane** is the most compressed interior in the house — a low loggia under a 6'0" soffit — opening to the largest sky-view on the property.

**Terrace C:**
- 600 sq ft, oriented north to the hemlock forest
- A stone-paved outdoor room, not a conventional roof
- The parapet is a planter, full-width, continuous — no gap between building edge and living soil
- Drainage channels cut directly into the concrete, stained red-orange by iron mineral runoff

**The view from here:**
Standing on Terrace C, you can look down through the staggered trays to Bear Run below. All three cantilevers are visible simultaneously. The section — which Wright drew in two hours on a September morning in 1935 — is fully legible for the first and only time.

**Legacy note:** Fallingwater was named the greatest work of American architecture by the AIA in 1991. It receives ~170,000 visitors per year. The structural restoration (post-tensioning, 2002) added an estimated 200 years to its service life.

> "The reality of the building is not the walls and the roof, but the space within."
> — Wright, paraphrasing Lao Tzu

title: Fault Tree Cascade - API Latency Incident
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: depth
title: Fault Tree Cascade - API Latency Incident
view: stack
category: Engineering
---

An incident review as a layered fault tree. Each depth moves from symptom to
mechanism to prevention.

@plane z=0 label="Symptom"
# Symptom

```
checkout-api p95 latency

10:04  220 ms
10:08  410 ms
10:12  960 ms
10:16  1800 ms
10:20  2400 ms
```

Users saw slow checkout confirmation pages. Error rate stayed low, which made
the incident look smaller than it felt.

@plane z=1 label="Immediate Cause"
# Immediate Cause

```
checkout-api
    |
    v
inventory-read
    |
    v
primary postgres

pool wait: 1430 ms
```

Connection pool wait dominated request time. Threads were not blocked; they
were waiting politely in a very long line.

@plane z=2 label="Contributing Factors"
# Contributing Factors

```
               promo traffic +42%
                    |
cache TTL cut 15m -> 2m
                    |
read replica lag 38s
                    |
fallback to primary
```

No single change broke the system. The cascade needed traffic, lower cache
life, and replica lag at the same time.

@plane z=3 label="Control Breaks"
# Control Breaks

```
alerting gap
  p95 page latency: yes
  DB pool wait:     no
  replica lag:      delayed

runbook gap
  safe cache TTL rollback: missing
```

The team knew the page was slow before it knew why. That cost nine minutes.

@plane z=4 label="Fix Stack"
# Fix Stack

```
1. Add pool-wait SLO
2. Pin promo TTL floor at 10m
3. Fail closed on replica lag over 10s
4. Add one-command TTL rollback
5. Load test promo profile weekly
```

The fix is a stack, too: detect earlier, prevent the risky combination, and
make rollback boring.

title: Fireworks Burst
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: frame
title: Fireworks Burst
fps: 24
loop: false
palette: gold-on-navy
---
A single aerial shell, drawn frame by frame. Read the planes in `z` order to
watch one chrysanthemum break: the shell climbs, the charge ignites, a sphere of
gold stars expands, gravity bends the trails into a willow, and the embers fade
to smoke. Each plane is one keyframe; the `x`/`y` offsets track the burst center
as it drifts on the wind.

@plane z=0 label="Launch" x=0 y=-40
# Frame 0 - Launch

The mortar fires. A single comet of packed black powder leaves the tube and
rises on a thin orange tail. Nothing has broken yet.

- **Altitude:** climbing, roughly 1/3 of apex
- **Visible:** one bright point, a faint smoke trail below it
- **Sound:** the dull *thump* of the lift charge
- **Color:** warm orange, almost white at the head

The fuse is timed to reach the burst charge near the top of the arc. Hold this
point of light steady; everything after this is the break.

Next: the shell coasts to its peak in [[z=1|the apex]].

@plane z=1 label="Apex" x=2 y=10
# Frame 1 - Apex

The comet slows at the top of its climb. For a fraction of a second it hangs,
nearly motionless, the tail thinning to almost nothing. Wind has nudged the
center about two units to the right.

This is the quiet beat before the burst. The timing fuse has just reached the
internal charge.

- **Velocity:** near zero (the still moment)
- **Visible:** a single dim ember, no trail
- **Tension:** maximum, the audience holds its breath

> A good shell breaks at apex, where it is slowest, so the sphere stays round
> instead of smearing into a teardrop.

Jump back to [[z=0|the launch]], or forward to [[z=2|ignition]].

@plane z=2 label="Ignition" x=2 y=12
# Frame 2 - Ignition

The burst charge detonates. The shell's casing splits and the packed stars are
thrown outward in every direction at once. This is the brightest single frame of
the whole sequence.

- **Flash:** a hard white core, blown-out for one frame
- **Stars:** dozens of points launching radially outward
- **Radius:** small but growing fast
- **Sound:** arrives late, the *crack* the crowd feels in the chest

Render this frame with a short, intense highlight. The next frame is all about
the symmetric spread.

On to [[z=3|the full bloom]].

@plane z=3 label="Bloom" x=3 y=12
# Frame 3 - Full Bloom

The chrysanthemum opens. Every star traces a straight radial line from the
center, forming a near-perfect sphere of gold. This is the iconic shape, the
frame you would put on a poster.

| Property   | Value                        |
|------------|------------------------------|
| Shape      | sphere (chrysanthemum break) |
| Star count | ~64 visible trails           |
| Color      | saturated gold, even falloff |
| Radius     | maximum extent               |

Each trail is the same length, which is what makes the burst read as round. The
core has already gone dark; only the expanding shell of stars remains.

From here gravity takes over. See [[z=4|the willow droop]].

@plane z=4 label="Willow" x=4 y=4
# Frame 4 - Willow Droop

Expansion stops. The stars have spent their outward energy, and now gravity
curves every trail downward. The crisp sphere softens into the drooping
"willow" form, golden strands raining toward the ground.

- **Motion:** outward speed gone, downward speed building
- **Shape:** sphere relaxing into a hanging dome
- **Color:** gold cooling toward amber at the trail tips
- **Trails:** longer now, with visible fade from head to tail

This is the lingering, graceful part of the effect, the part that earns the
*ooh* a beat after the *crack*.

Compare with [[z=3|the bloom]], or watch it die in [[z=5|the fade]].

@plane z=5 label="Fade" x=5 y=-6
# Frame 5 - Fade to Smoke

The stars burn out one by one. The bright amber tips wink off, leaving only
faint glowing embers and a drifting cloud of smoke lit from below by the next
shell on the ground.

- **Brightness:** near zero, a few stray sparks
- **Visible:** gray-blue smoke, a couple of dying embers
- **Color:** desaturated, mostly the navy of the night sky again
- **Sound:** silence, then the lift *thump* of the next shell

The frame returns almost to where it began: a dark sky with one small point of
light. If `loop` were true, this is where it would cut back to
[[z=0|the launch]] and start over.

*End of burst.*

title: Hand-Blown Vase: Gather to Anneal
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: step
title: Hand-Blown Vase: Gather to Anneal
view: present
glass: borosilicate, COE 33
furnace_temp: 2100°F
annealer_temp: 960°F
blowtube: 5/8" stainless, 60"
estimated_time: 45 min
category: Home
---
A hand-blown vase from first gather to the annealer — each step a plane, each plane a breath.

@plane z=0 label="The Gather"
# First Gather

Dip the **cold blowtube** into the furnace at a **45° angle**, rotating steadily at ~2 rpm. Pull a gather of roughly **300g** of molten soda-lime glass — it should glow **bright orange**, almost white at the surface.

**Feel for:** uniform heat across the gather. Any dark streaks mean cold spots — reheat in the glory hole before proceeding.

> "The gather is a promise. A cold spot is a broken one."

- Rotate continuously — gravity is always pulling
- Target gather diameter: **5–6 cm**
- Time in furnace: **8–12 seconds**

[[z=1|Ready to marver →]]

@plane z=1 label="Marvering"
# Marver the Gather

Roll the gather on the **steel marver table**, applying gentle downward pressure to:

1. Cool the outer skin slightly (makes it workable)
2. Center the mass on the pipe
3. Consolidate any bubbles toward the tip

**Marver pattern:**
```
← roll 3× →  then  ↺ rotate 90°  then  ← roll 3× →
```

Target shape after marvering: a **smooth teardrop**, tip pointing away from you, ~7 cm long.

- Do NOT press hard — you want even skin, not a flat spot
- Return to the **glory hole** at 2050°F for 20–30 seconds if the glass stiffens
- Skin temp after marvering: ~1400°F

[[z=2|First blow →]]

@plane z=2 label="First Blow"
# Opening the Bubble

Cap the end of the tube with your **thumb**, then place your lips on the mouthpiece. One **slow, steady breath** — about 2 psi — to open the initial bubble.

**What you're looking for:**
- A small, even dome forming at the tip (~2 cm diameter)
- No lopsided bulge (rotate if it pulls to one side)
- The glass should still glow **deep orange**

> Do NOT blow hard. The glass does the work. You are just suggesting.

| Mistake | Fix |
|---|---|
| Bubble off-center | Reheat and gravity-correct with pipe angle |
| Bubble collapsed | Glass too cold — back to glory hole |
| Too large too fast | You over-blew — marver back down |

[[z=3|Shaping the neck →]]

@plane z=3 label="Neck & Shoulder"
# Pulling the Neck

With **jacks** (V-shaped graphite tool), score a **neck** ~8 cm from the blowtube end while rotating. Apply steady inward pressure at ~1400°F to pinch the shoulder.

**The neck defines everything.** Too thin too early and the vase will collapse under its own weight. Target neck diameter: **2.8 cm**.

After jacking the neck, use a **block** (wet cherry wood, 10 cm bowl) to cup and round the **shoulder** below it:

```
      [blowtube]
         |
    _____|_____    ← neck: 2.8 cm
   /           \   ← shoulder
  |             |  ← body (expanding)
   \___________/
```

Reheat in glory hole: **25 seconds**, constant rotation. Then [[z=4|blow the body →]].

@plane z=4 label="Blowing the Body"
# Expanding the Body

Now the real blow. Tilt the pipe **downward at 30°** — gravity assists the expansion — and deliver a **sustained breath** over 3–4 seconds while rotating.

**Target dimensions:**
- Body diameter: **12 cm**
- Body height (below neck): **18 cm**
- Wall thickness: **3–4 mm** (hold up to a light — even glow = even walls)

Gravity + rotation + breath work together. If one side thickens, tilt the pipe that side up and let gravity thin it out. Three reheats in the glory hole are normal here.

> The vase is not made — it is coaxed.

Check wall thickness by holding the glowing form in front of the bench light. Dark bands = thick spots. Rotate and reheat those areas selectively.

[[z=5|Color & detail →]]

@plane z=5 label="Color & Detail"
# Adding Color

Return the hot vase body to the marver and roll across **crushed dichroic frit** (pre-laid in a 15 cm strip). The frit adheres instantly at surface temp >1300°F.

- **Frit used:** copper ruby + silver amber, 80/20 mix
- **Marver roll:** 2 passes, 180° apart
- **Reheat:** 30 seconds at 2000°F to melt frit flush with surface

Optional: use a **pick** (tungsten rod) to drag color lines while the glass is at 1600°F — one long pull from shoulder to base creates an elegant **feathered streak**.

```
Surface after frit + pick drag:
 ≋≋≋≋≋≋≋   ← amber streaks
≋≋≋≋≋≋≋≋≋
 ≋≋≋≋≋≋≋   ← ruby base
```

Two more glory-hole passes to fully integrate color. Then [[z=6|open the lip →]].

@plane z=6 label="Opening the Lip"
# Flaring the Lip

Score a **transfer mark** just above the neck with a wet iron rod, then use a **punty** (solid iron rod with a small glass blob) to attach to the **base** of the vase.

Tap the score line — the vase transfers to the punty. Now you can open the top:

1. Reheat the lip end to **bright orange** (1600°F)
2. Insert **jacks** into the opening and gently spread — 3 mm per pass
3. Target lip diameter: **9 cm**, flared at ~15° outward
4. Optionally use a **paddle** to flatten the lip plane for a clean horizontal edge

> This is the moment the vase becomes a vase. Before this, it was just a bubble.

Final lip thickness: **2.5 mm**. Smooth, even, no drips at rim. Knock off the punty with a sharp tap. The piece drops into the **waiting ceramic fiber blanket**.

[[z=7|Into the annealer →]]

@plane z=7 label="Annealing"
# Annealing: The Long Cool

Place the vase immediately into the **annealer kiln**, preheated to **960°F** (the strain point of soda-lime glass). This temperature relieves internal stress built up during shaping.

**Annealing schedule:**

| Phase | Temp | Duration |
|---|---|---|
| Soak | 960°F | 30 min |
| Slow cool | 960 → 700°F | 90 min |
| Fast cool | 700 → 400°F | 60 min |
| Room temp | 400 → 70°F | 8 hrs |

**Total annealing time: ~10 hours.** Open kiln door only after glass is below 100°F.

Glass that skips annealing will shatter from thermal shock — sometimes hours later, sometimes days, always dramatically.

> Tomorrow morning, the vase will be waiting. That is the glassblower's patience — you make it today, you have it tomorrow.

**Finished piece:** 21 cm tall, 12 cm wide, copper-ruby dichroic fade, feathered amber pull, 2 mm flared lip. One of a kind.

title: A Year in the Hive
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: season
title: A Year in the Hive
view: present
colony: Langstroth 10-frame, overwintered double-deep
location: Zone 6b, Upper Midwest
category: Education
---
Follow one colony — roughly 60,000 bees at peak — through the wheel of the year.

@plane z=0 label="Winter"
# Winter — The Cluster
**December through February**

The colony has contracted to a tight **winter cluster**, about the size of a volleyball, pressing against comb cells full of stored honey. There are no drones. The worker population has dropped to ~10,000 — but these are *fat-bodied winter bees*, loaded with vitellogenin protein, built to live 6 months instead of 6 weeks.

The cluster generates heat by vibrating their flight muscles without moving their wings. Core temperature stays at **93 °F (34 °C)** even when it is −20 °F outside.

> The bees do not hibernate. They are awake, burning through ~25 lb of honey between October and March.

**What to watch:**
- [ ] Heft the hive — if it feels light (< 40 lb), emergency feed fondant
- [ ] Entrance reducer in place — blocks mice, not airflow
- [ ] No peeking before 45 °F days

---
*Next: [[z=1|Spring buildup begins the moment the queen lays again]]*

@plane z=1 label="Early Spring"
# Early Spring — The Hunger Gap
**March through April**

The queen began laying in January — just a few hundred eggs a day — and now the cluster is expanding fast. This is the **most dangerous month**. Brood must be kept at 95 °F, which burns honey faster than summer, but there is almost nothing blooming yet.

**Population curve:**
| Month | Approximate Workers |
|-------|-------------------|
| Feb   | 8,000 |
| Mar   | 18,000 |
| Apr   | 35,000 |
| May   | 55,000 |

The beekeeper's job right now: **do not disturb**, but do feed.

A 1:1 sugar syrup (1 lb sugar : 1 lb water) mimics nectar and triggers comb-building. Pollen substitute patties — compressed powder of soy flour, brewers yeast, and real pollen — feed the nurse bees so they can produce royal jelly for the larvae.

> First dandelion sighted = first real pollen flight of the year. The foragers come home with **bright orange saddle-bags** on their hind legs.

---
*Next: [[z=2|The swarm impulse arrives with the main nectar flow]]*

@plane z=2 label="Late Spring"
# Late Spring — The Swarm Impulse
**May through June**

The hive is at capacity: 60,000 bees, brood in every frame, nectar coming in faster than it can be cured. The colony does what it evolved to do — **it prepares to divide**.

Workers build **swarm cells**: large, peanut-shaped queen cells hanging from the bottom of frames. When the first cell is capped, the old queen leaves with roughly half the workers in a **prime swarm**. They boil out of the entrance in a roaring cloud, circle for 20 minutes, and land in a temporary bivouac — often a tree branch — while scouts search for a new cavity.

The swarm hangs, surprisingly docile, for 1 to 3 days.

**Swarm prevention checklist:**
- [ ] Add a honey super before frames are 80% full
- [ ] Split the hive: move the old queen + 3 frames of brood to a new box
- [ ] Destroy all but one swarm cell in the parent hive (leave the best)
- [ ] Re-queen with a bought queen if genetics are poor

> A swarm is the colony's *reproductive success* — from the bees' perspective, everything else was just preparation for this moment.

---
*Next: [[z=3|The nectar flow — the reason beekeepers keep bees]]*

@plane z=3 label="Summer"
# Summer — The Flow
**June through August**

White clover, basswood, black locust, goldenrod. The major nectar sources vary by region, but for 4 to 8 weeks, foragers make **up to 10 trips per day**, each returning with a 40 mg droplet of nectar in her honey stomach.

**From nectar to honey — the numbers:**
- 1 lb of honey requires ~5 lb of nectar
- 5 lb of nectar requires ~20,000 forager trips
- A strong hive can cap **3–5 lb of honey per day** during a hard flow

Back in the hive, **house bees** receive the nectar, add enzymes (invertase, glucose oxidase), and spread it in open cells. Fanners stand at the entrance and beat their wings for hours, evaporating moisture from 80% down to **17–18% water** — the threshold where honey is shelf-stable forever.

> A capped super frame holds about **6 lb of honey**. A full 10-frame honey super: ~60 lb.

**Mite check:** Varroa destructor populations double every 28 days during brood season. Do an **alcohol wash** (300 bees, count mites): treat if > 2 mites per 100 bees.

---
*Next: [[z=4|The colony prepares for another winter]]*

@plane z=4 label="Fall"
# Fall — The Reckoning
**September through November**

The goldenrod finishes. Drone eviction begins: workers bite the legs of the few hundred remaining drones and drag them to the entrance. Drones cannot sting, cannot forage, contribute nothing to winter survival. They die on the landing board within hours.

The queen is laying **winter bees** now — the same long-lived, fat-bodied workers who will carry the colony through to March. She will reduce laying to near-zero by November.

**The beekeeper's fall checklist:**
- [ ] Harvest honey supers (leave 60–80 lb for the bees in the deeps)
- [ ] Treat for Varroa: oxalic acid dribble or vaporization works when brood is minimal
- [ ] Combine weak colonies — a small colony cannot generate enough cluster heat
- [ ] Mouse guard on the entrance: 3/8-inch mesh strip, stapled
- [ ] Insulate the top if your winters go below 0 °F for weeks

> The colony you take into winter is the colony you get in spring. There is no recovering from a bad fall.

---
*Back to [[z=0|the cluster waiting out the cold]]*

title: API Gateway Outage - On-Call Runbook
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: step
title: API Gateway Outage - On-Call Runbook
category: Engineering
schema: runbook/1
entrypoint: "0"
resolved: "7"
service: api-gateway
severity: sev2
view: single
---
One file, two readers. A human on-call follows the prose and clicks the branch
that matches reality. A program parses the same planes as a decision graph and
can dry-run, lint for dead ends, or drive an auto-remediation. Each [[z=N|...]]
link is a branch; step 0 is the entrypoint, step 7 is resolution.

@plane z=0 label="DETECT" kind=entry signal="5xx_rate>5%"
# Detect

PagerDuty fired: `5xx rate > 5% for 3m` on `api-gateway`. Open the dashboard and
confirm it is real, not one bad host or a deploy still rolling.

- error rate truly elevated -> [[z=1|TRIAGE]]
- single host or already recovering -> [[z=7|RESOLVED]]

@plane z=1 label="TRIAGE" kind=decision
# Triage

Find the blast radius. `kubectl get pods -n prod | grep -v Running` and pull the
last deploy time. Decide the most likely cause.

- a deploy shipped in the last 30m -> [[z=3|ROLLBACK]]
- DB latency or connection errors in the logs -> [[z=2|DB_CHECK]]
- cause unclear after 10m -> [[z=6|ESCALATE]]

@plane z=2 label="DB_CHECK" kind=decision tool="psql"
# Database check

Check the primary: `SELECT count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity;`, replication lag,
slow-query log.

- DB is saturated or lagging -> [[z=4|MITIGATE_DB]]
- DB looks healthy -> [[z=3|ROLLBACK]]

@plane z=3 label="ROLLBACK" kind=action cmd="kubectl rollout undo"
# Roll back the last deploy

`kubectl rollout undo deploy/api-gateway -n prod`. Watch the error rate for 5m.

- errors dropping -> [[z=5|VERIFY]]
- no improvement -> [[z=6|ESCALATE]]

@plane z=4 label="MITIGATE_DB" kind=action
# Mitigate the database

Kill long-running queries, fail over to the replica if the primary is wedged,
then widen the connection pool.

- recovered -> [[z=5|VERIFY]]
- still degraded -> [[z=6|ESCALATE]]

@plane z=5 label="VERIFY" kind=decision
# Verify recovery

Error rate under 1% for 5m, latency normal, the request queue drained.

- all green -> [[z=7|RESOLVED]]
- still red -> [[z=6|ESCALATE]]

@plane z=6 label="ESCALATE" kind=action
# Escalate

Page the incident commander and the service owner. Post the timeline in
`#incident`. Take the directed action, then re-verify.

- mitigation applied -> [[z=5|VERIFY]]

@plane z=7 label="RESOLVED" kind=terminal accept=true
# Resolved

Metrics stable. Close the incident, snapshot the timeline, schedule the
postmortem. The on-call's job is done.

title: Welwyn North Interlocking — Down Fast to Platform 2
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: step
title: Welwyn North Interlocking — Down Fast to Platform 2
view: present
signalbox: Welwyn North SB (NX panel, 1959)
route: 1D77 King's Cross → Cambridge, Down Fast → Platform 2
timetable: 14:23 pass, 0 min late
operator: Leif H., Monday turn
category: Engineering
---
You are setting road for 1D77 through Welwyn North. Seven interlocking steps, one keystroke at a time. Each plane is a discrete state the frame must pass through before the next can be entered.

@plane z=0 label="Idle — Line Clear"

## 🟩 All Normal, Line Clear

The panel is quiet. Berth track circuits show white. Signal WN15 (Down Fast home) stands at **Danger — Red**.

```
 WN13  WN15        WN17
  🟥    🟥          🟥
   |     |           |
===⬛====⬛===[23]===⬛===
         DF          P2
         ^
      1D77 approaching (1.2 miles)
```

- All points: normal (N)
- Track circuits: clear
- Lock: no routes set

> Bell received from Hitchin: **2-1** (Express Passenger approaching). Acknowledged with **2-1**.

---
**Next action:** Set points 23A/23B to Platform 2 road. [[z=1|Step 1 →]]

@plane z=1 label="Points Normal → Reverse"

## ⚙️ Points 23A and 23B — Calling Over

Operate point switches **23A** (trailing) then **23B** (facing) on the NX panel.

| Point | Normal position | Required | Movement |
|-------|----------------|----------|----------|
| 23A   | Down Fast through | Reverse | Swing trailing end toward P2 |
| 23B   | Down Fast through | Reverse | Swing facing end toward P2 |

```
Before:          After:
 DF ──────────    DF ──────┐
         P2 │              P2
            │
```

- Points motor runs: **4.2 seconds**
- Detection lights: red (moving) → **white (detected, reversed)**
- Facing point lock energised: **FPL 23B confirmed**

> **Warning:** If 23B detection fails, route cannot be set. Check point heaters — last frost 06:14 this morning.

[[z=0|← Back]] · [[z=2|Step 2 →]]

@plane z=2 label="Overlap Check"

## 🔒 Overlap — TC24 Must Be Clear

The interlocking will not release the proceed aspect until the **overlap track circuit TC24** (80 metres beyond WN17, the starting signal) is proved clear.

```
WN17 (starting)
  |
  |——— 80m overlap ———|
  |                   |
 [23]               [TC24]
                       ^
               Must show WHITE (clear)
               before WN15 can clear
```

- TC24 status: **CLEAR** ✓
- Overlap route locked in rear of WN17
- Conflicting route via WN19 (Up Slow cross-over): **locked out** automatically

> The 14:19 Up Slow (2C44) cleared TC24 at 14:21:08. Two minutes and counting — plenty of room.

[[z=1|← Back]] · [[z=3|Step 3 →]]

@plane z=3 label="Route Set — Signals Clear"

## 🟢 Route Proved, Signals Clear

Interlocking logic satisfied. Press **WN15 signal button** — route lights up on the panel, signal clears.

```
 WN13  WN15        WN17
  🟥    🟢          🟡
   |     |           |
===⬛====⬛===[23R]==⬛===
         DF          P2
                ^
           route lit (yellow)
```

- WN15: **Proceed — Double Yellow** (two-aspect clear)
- WN17: **Caution — Single Yellow** (platform starter, held at danger until 1D77 booked stop)
- Points 23A/23B: **locked reversed** for duration of route
- Route indicator at WN15: **"2"** (feather, lit)

> The driver will see double yellow at WN15 and ease off for the platform starter.

[[z=2|← Back]] · [[z=4|Step 4 →]]

@plane z=4 label="Train Entering Section"

## 🚆 1D77 Fouling the Berth

14:23:41 — 1D77 hits **TC20** (approach berth). Signal description populates automatically on the panel.

```
 TC20  TC21   TC22  [23R] TC23
  🟫    ⬛      ⬛           ⬛
         |
      1D77 here
      Class 387, 8-car, 163m
      Driver: reported on time
```

- TC20: **occupied** (red)
- WN15 clears to single yellow as train passes (track-circuit replacement)
- Route remains locked — **cannot be cancelled** while train is on route

> Standard. You watch the berths walk the train down the panel. Satisfying, like a bead on a wire.

[[z=3|← Back]] · [[z=5|Step 5 →]]

@plane z=5 label="Platform Occupied"

## 🛑 Platform 2 — Train Standing

14:24:09 — 1D77 comes to a stand. TC23 occupied, TC22 occupied. WN17 holds at **Danger**.

```
         [23R]  TC23    P2 platform
                 🟫🟫🟫🟫🟫🟫🟫🟫
                 |← 163m Class 387 →|

WN17: 🟥  (platform starter — DANGER)
```

- Route automatically **replaced** once all berths behind WN17 re-clear
- Points 23A/23B: still locked reversed
- Station call: **1 min 30 sec** scheduled

> Station supervisor presses the "right away" plunger at 14:25:38. One pip on the bell, driver acknowledges two toots.

[[z=4|← Back]] · [[z=6|Step 6 →]]

@plane z=6 label="Departure — Route Released"

## 🟢 Right Away — WN17 Clears

14:25:40 — "Right Away" received. Clear WN17 (Down Platform 2 starter) for onward section to Knebworth.

```
WN17 (starter, P2)
  🟢  PROCEED
   |
   |——— TC24 overlap still held ———|
   |
  TC24: CLEAR ✓
  Section to Knebworth: LINE CLEAR (received 14:24:55)
```

- WN17: **Proceed — Green** (line clear to Knebworth home)
- Train departs 14:25:42 (**2 minutes late** — boarding overrun)
- As 1D77 clears TC23: points 23A/23B **release**, motor swings back to **Normal**
- Route extinguishes on panel — back to [[z=0|idle state]]

> Log entry: `14:23 1D77 arr P2, 14:25 dep, 2L. Pts 23 op x2. No irregularities. WNS.`

The panel goes quiet again. Next working: 14:31, the Up Fast empty stock from Cambridge.

|---|---|

| Radar 2 | go |

```
stage 1:  green
stage 2:  green

```

RP-1 system:  stable

```

Ground is go.

```
weather  -> go
range    -> go
vehicle  -> go with watch

poll result: GO

The word is small because the machine underneath it is large.

title: Wildflower Road - Song Chart
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: section
title: Wildflower Road - Song Chart
key: G major
tempo: "92 bpm"
time_signature: "4/4"
capo: "2"
---
A full song chart laid out by section. Each plane is one section of the
arrangement; scrub the Z axis to walk through the form from intro to outro. Chords
are given relative to the capo (capo 2, so shapes in G sound in A). Strum pattern
unless noted: D DU UDU.

@plane z=0 label="Intro"
# Intro (4 bars)
Fingerpicked, no vocals. Sets the mood.

```
| G   | Cadd9 | G   | D/F# |
```

Let the last D/F# ring, then the band comes in on [[z=1|the first verse]].

@plane z=1 label="Verse 1"
# Verse 1 (8 bars)
Full strum enters here.

```
| G        | Cadd9    | G        | D        |
| Em       | C        | G    D   | G        |
```

Lyric: "Down the wildflower road where the gravel turns to gold..."

@plane z=2 label="Pre-Chorus"
# Pre-Chorus (4 bars)
Build. Hold back the strum, palm-muted eighths.

```
| Em       | C        | G        | D        |
```

Crescendo into the downbeat of [[z=3|the chorus]].

@plane z=3 label="Chorus"
# Chorus (8 bars)
Open up, full voicings, harmonies on the title line.

```
| C        | G        | D        | Em       |
| C        | G        | D        | G        |
```

Lyric hook: "And I'll carry you home, carry you home."

@plane z=4 label="Verse 2"
# Verse 2 (8 bars)
Same chords as [[z=1|Verse 1]]. Add a tambourine on beats 2 and 4.

```
| G        | Cadd9    | G        | D        |
| Em       | C        | G    D   | G        |
```

@plane z=5 label="Bridge"
# Bridge (6 bars)
New harmonic color. Drop to a single guitar and voice for the first two bars.

```
| Am       | Em       | C        | D        |
| Am       | D        |
```

Big ritardando on the last D, then crash back into the final chorus.

@plane z=6 label="Final Chorus"
# Final Chorus (8 bars)
Repeat [[z=3|the chorus]] twice, second time up a step in energy. Everyone sings.

@plane z=7 label="Outro"
# Outro (4 bars)
Wind down to the intro figure, fingerpicked again.

```
| G   | Cadd9 | G   | G |
```

Final chord: let G ring and fade.
