---

A go/no-go poll as an operational loop. Each plane shows one minute of the room
as the team converges on a Trans-Lunar Injection burn.

@plane z=0 label="T minus 5"
# T minus 5 - Systems Quiet

```
FLIGHT LOOP

GUIDO   green
FIDO    green
EECOM   green
GNC     watching drift
RETRO   green
CAPCOM  standby
```

Telemetry is stable. Guidance sees a small drift but it is inside limits.

@plane z=1 label="T minus 4"
# T minus 4 - Drift Check

```
attitude error

pitch  +0.03 deg  OK
yaw    +0.07 deg  watch
roll   -0.02 deg  OK
```

GNC asks for one more sample before giving a clean go. Flight holds the poll.

@plane z=2 label="T minus 3"
# T minus 3 - Go Poll Opens

```
FLIGHT: "Give me your go for TLI."

GUIDO  GO
FIDO   GO
EECOM  GO
GNC    GO, drift damping
RETRO  GO
```

The room tightens around one answer. Nobody speaks twice unless the data
changes.

@plane z=3 label="T minus 2"
# T minus 2 - Commit Criteria

```
burn gate

state vector      locked
engine gimbal     armed
abort corridor    valid
ground uplink     closed
crew checklist    complete
```

Every gate has an owner. Every owner has a number.

@plane z=4 label="T minus 1"
# T minus 1 - CAPCOM Call

```
CAPCOM to crew:

"You are go for TLI."

crew:
"Roger, go for TLI."
```

The loop collapses into one sentence. That is the beauty of the poll.

@plane z=5 label="Ignition"
# Ignition

```
S-IVB burn

00:00 ignition
00:12 chamber stable
01:20 guidance holding
05:48 cutoff predicted
```

The room moves from decision to watch mode. A good go poll leaves no loose
threads behind.

title: The Lunar Cycle, Phase by Phase
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: time
title: The Lunar Cycle, Phase by Phase
synodic_period: 29.53 days
hemisphere: northern
illumination_note: percentages are approximate mid-phase values
---
The Moon does not make its own light. Every phase you see is sunlight reflected
off the half of the Moon that faces the Sun, viewed from a slowly shifting angle
as the Moon orbits Earth. One full trip through the named phases is a synodic
month of about 29.53 days, measured from one New Moon to the next.

Read the planes below in Z order: time runs from day 0 (New Moon) forward through
one complete cycle. The waxing half (growing light) sits to the left of center,
the waning half (shrinking light) to the right, so the stack mirrors the way the
terminator sweeps across the disc.

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="New Moon"
# New Moon (day 0)

The Moon sits between Earth and the Sun, so its lit face points away from us and
the disc is effectively invisible. Illumination near 0 percent.

- **Rises:** around sunrise
- **Sets:** around sunset
- **Visible:** essentially not; lost in the Sun's glare
- **Tides:** spring tides (Sun and Moon pull in line)

This is the only moment a solar eclipse can happen, and only when the alignment
is exact enough for the Moon's shadow to touch Earth. From here the lit edge
begins to creep into view. Continue to [[z=3.7|the Waxing Crescent]].

@plane z=3.7 x=-2 y=1 label="Waxing Crescent"
# Waxing Crescent (about day 3 to 4)

A slim, curved sliver of light appears on the **right** side of the disc as seen
from the northern hemisphere. Illumination roughly 1 to 49 percent and climbing.

- **Best seen:** in the western sky just after sunset
- **Earthshine:** the dark portion often glows faintly, sunlight bounced off
  Earth and back onto the Moon (sometimes called "the old Moon in the new Moon's
  arms")

The lit fraction grows a little each evening. When the disc reaches exactly half
lit, we call it First Quarter: jump ahead to [[z=7.4|First Quarter]].

@plane z=7.4 x=-1 y=0 label="First Quarter"
# First Quarter (about day 7 to 8)

Half the disc is lit, the right half from the northern view. Despite looking like
a "half moon," it is called First Quarter because the Moon has completed one
quarter of its orbit. Illumination near 50 percent.

- **Rises:** around noon
- **Sets:** around midnight
- **Visible:** high in the southern sky in the evening, a great time for binocular
  viewing along the **terminator** (the day/night line) where crater shadows are
  longest

Light keeps spilling across the disc, swelling into the bulging Waxing Gibbous
over the next several nights until the whole near side blazes at
[[z=14.8|Full Moon]].

@plane z=14.8 x=0 y=2 label="Full Moon"
# Full Moon (about day 14 to 15)

Earth now sits between the Sun and the Moon, so the full near side is lit and the
disc is a complete bright circle. Illumination near 100 percent.

- **Rises:** around sunset
- **Sets:** around sunrise
- **Visible:** all night, the brightest natural object in the night sky
- **Tides:** spring tides again (Sun and Moon aligned, opposite sides)

A lunar eclipse can occur only at Full Moon, when Earth's shadow falls across the
disc. After tonight the light begins to retreat from the right edge, thinning
through the Waning Gibbous toward [[z=22.1|Last Quarter]].

@plane z=22.1 x=2 y=0 label="Last Quarter"
# Last Quarter (about day 22 to 23)

Half lit again, but now the **left** half glows (northern view), the opposite
side from First Quarter. The Moon has completed three quarters of its orbit.
Illumination near 50 percent.

- **Rises:** around midnight
- **Sets:** around noon
- **Visible:** high in the sky before dawn and into the morning

The terminator once more throws dramatic shadows for observers, this time across
the opposite landscape. From here only a fading curve remains: finish with
[[z=29.53|the Waning Crescent]].

@plane z=29.53 x=2 y=1 label="Waning Crescent"
# Waning Crescent (about day 25 to 28)

A thin curve of light clings to the **left** edge and thins night by night.
Illumination roughly 49 down toward 0 percent. Earthshine often returns, softly
lighting the dark portion just before dawn.

- **Best seen:** in the eastern sky just before sunrise
- **Sometimes called:** the "old Moon"

When the last sliver dissolves into the Sun's glare, the Moon returns to the
alignment it started from and the synodic month closes. The next New Moon begins
the cycle anew: loop back to [[z=0|New Moon]].

title: Museum of the Anthropocene
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: room
title: Museum of the Anthropocene
view: present
legend: E=🚪 .=⬜ W=🟫 A=🟦 I=🟧 F=🟥 S=🟩 G=⬛ X=🔲 C=🟪 D=🟨 T=⬜
curator: Dr. Maren Solvik
floors: 1
opened: 2031
category: Maps
---

Welcome to the **Museum of the Anthropocene** — a self-guided journey through six rooms documenting humanity's mark on the planet. Use the legend map below to orient yourself, then step room by room through the exhibit.

@plane z=0 label="Floor Map"

## Floor Plan — Ground Level

```
W W W W W W W W W W W W W W W W W W W W W
W . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . W
W . A A A A . I I I I . F F F F . S S . W
W . A A A A E I I I I E F F F F E S S . W
W . A A A A . I I I I . F F F F . S S . W
W . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . W
W . G G G G . . . . . . C C C C . . . . W
W . G G G G E . . . D D C C C C . . . . W
W . G G G G . . . . D D C C C C . . . . W
W . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . W
W W W W W W W W W W W W W W W E W W W W W
```

**Legend**
| Symbol | Room |
|--------|------|
| 🟦 A | Room 1 — Ocean Archive |
| 🟧 I | Room 2 — Ice Memory |
| 🟥 F | Room 3 — Fire Season |
| 🟩 S | Room 4 — Species Vault |
| ⬛ G | Room 5 — Ground Zero |
| 🟪 C | Room 6 — Carbon Chapel |
| 🟨 D | Reading Alcove |
| 🚪 E | Doorways |

> Start at the south entrance (bottom center) and proceed clockwise.

@plane z=1 label="Room 1 — Ocean Archive"

## Room 1 — Ocean Archive

```
W W W W W W W W W W
W A A A A A A A A W
W A A A A A A A A W
W A A A A A A A A W
W W W W E W W W W W
```

*Sea-level recordings from 1880 to 2031.*

The walls here are floor-to-ceiling glass tanks filled with Atlantic seawater at three acidity levels — **1880**, **1980**, and **today**. Watch coral fragments placed in each tank: the rightmost dissolves visibly over the course of your visit.

**Highlights**
- [ ] Touch the pH tiles along the north wall
- [ ] Listen to the hydrophone feed: open ocean, 1963 vs. 2031
- [ ] Read the message-in-a-bottle station — notes from coastal villages now underwater

> *"The sea remembers what the land forgets."* — wall inscription, east alcove

**Key data point:** Average sea-level rise since 1900: **+21 cm**. Projected by 2100: **+0.6 to 1.1 m**.

Proceed east through the doorway → [[z=2|Room 2 — Ice Memory]]

@plane z=2 label="Room 2 — Ice Memory"

## Room 2 — Ice Memory

```
W W W W E W W W W W
W I I I I I I I I W
W I I I I I I I I W
W I I I I I I I I W
W W W W E W W W W W
```

*An ice core library: 800,000 years of atmosphere, layered in cylinders of frozen time.*

The room is kept at **−4 °C** — coats are provided at the door. Each cylindrical case holds a meter-long ice core, lit from below in blue. Labels read like tree rings: dust from the Toba eruption, CO₂ spikes from industrialization, a pale ash band from 2023's Pacific megafires.

**Highlights**
- [ ] Hold the sample shard — feel a bubble of Pleistocene air trapped inside
- [ ] Trace the CO₂ graph etched into the floor in brass: flat for 10,000 years, then vertical
- [ ] Find the 1950 layer — it glows faintly from residual bomb-test isotopes

**Key data point:** Pre-industrial CO₂: **280 ppm**. June 2031: **428 ppm**.

→ [[z=3|Room 3 — Fire Season]]

@plane z=3 label="Room 3 — Fire Season"

## Room 3 — Fire Season

```
W W W W E W W W W W
W F F F F F F F F W
W F F F F F F F F W
W F F F F F F F F W
W W W W E W W W W W
```

*The world's largest curated archive of wildfire smoke.*

Amber resin panels line every wall — each panel casts the amber glow of a different mega-fire: Australia 2019, Siberia 2022, Amazon 2025. The scent (controlled diffuser) shifts as you move: eucalyptus char, boreal pine resin, wet ash.

**Highlights**
- [ ] The **Smoke Clock**: a mechanical display counting cumulative hectares burned since midnight today
- [ ] Satellite loop projected on the floor — 12 months of fire-scar spread, on repeat
- [ ] Interview booths: oral histories from three firefighters, three years apart, same forest

> The red door at the far end is sealed. A plaque reads: *"This room represents 2041 projections. Not yet open."*

**Key data point:** Global burned area trend: **+7% per decade** since 1980.

→ [[z=4|Room 4 — Species Vault]]

@plane z=4 label="Room 4 — Species Vault"

## Room 4 — Species Vault

```
W W W W E W W W W
W S S S S S S S W
W S S S S S S S W
W W W W W W W W W
```

*A cabinet of endings — and a few beginnings.*

Floor-to-ceiling drawers, museum-style, each labeled with a species. Pull any drawer: inside is a cast of the last-known specimen, a sound recording (if one exists), a scent capsule, and a single pressed plant or feather under glass.

**Highlights**
- [ ] Drawer **B-047**: the Bramble Cay melomys — first mammal declared extinct due to climate change, 2016
- [ ] The **Rewilding Wall**: 40 species brought back from the brink since 2010 — highlighted in gold
- [ ] Listen for the birdsong loop — 14 minutes of silence where extinct species' songs would fall

**Key data point:** Current extinction rate: **~1,000×** background rate. Rewilding successes since 2020: **214 species stabilized**.

→ [[z=5|Room 5 — Ground Zero]]

@plane z=5 label="Room 5 — Ground Zero"

## Room 5 — Ground Zero

```
W W W W W W W W W
W G G G G G G G W
W G G G G G G G W
W G G G G G G G W
W W W E W W W W W
```

*Soil. The most overlooked archive on Earth.*

The floor is glass — beneath it, a living cross-section of soil profiles from seven biomes, each column 2 meters deep and backlit. Roots, fungi, beetle galleries, earthworm tunnels: all visible. A time-lapse runs on the east wall: topsoil loss from a single Iowa field, 1930–2030.

**Highlights**
- [ ] Press the **Mycelium Panel** to watch a fungal network light up in real time (fluorescent spores)
- [ ] Smell Station: healthy forest humus vs. degraded agricultural soil — the difference is striking
- [ ] The **Debt Column**: a physical counter of tons of topsoil lost globally today — resets at midnight

> *"We know more about the surface of Mars than the top 30 cm beneath our feet."*

**Key data point:** Topsoil lost per year globally: **~24 billion tons**. Formation rate: **1 cm per 1,000 years**.

→ [[z=6|Room 6 — Carbon Chapel]]

@plane z=6 label="Room 6 — Carbon Chapel"

## Room 6 — Carbon Chapel

```
W W W E W W W W W
W C C C C C C C W
W C C D D C C C W
W C C D D C C C W
W W W W W W W W W
```

*The final room. A space for reckoning — and for choice.*

No artifacts here. The chapel is circular, with a single skylight. The walls display, in real time, a global CO₂ dashboard — emissions, drawdown, pledges, actuals. A reading alcove (🟨) holds **The Ledger**: a bound book visitors can write in, one sentence each, about what they will do.

**Highlights**
- [ ] Sit for five minutes — there are no prompts, no screens at eye level, only the sky
- [ ] The **Pledge Wall**: 40,000 handwritten cards from previous visitors, organized by year
- [ ] Write your entry in **The Ledger** before you leave

**Key data point:** As of today, **~2,300 GtCO₂** of the remaining carbon budget is left for a 1.5 °C pathway. Each year of current emissions: **~37 Gt**.

---

*Thank you for visiting the Museum of the Anthropocene. The gift shop carries only secondhand items. The café is zero-waste. The building is carbon-negative.*

**Exit** through the south door → back to [[z=0|Floor Map]]

title: Swinging Pendulum
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: frame
title: Swinging Pendulum
fps: 12
loop: ping-pong
length: 0.95 m
amplitude: 18 deg
---
A six-frame loop of a simple gravity pendulum released from the left.
Frames advance left to right, then play back in reverse to complete the swing.
The bob accelerates toward the bottom and decelerates at each turning point,
so the spacing between extreme frames is wide and the spacing near the
bottom is tight.

Pivot sits at the top center of every frame. Read the loop in order:
[[z=0|far left]] to [[z=2|bottom]] to [[z=4|far right]], then the viewer
reverses back through [[z=2|bottom]] again.

@plane z=0 x=-18 y=0 label="Frame 0: left extreme"
# Frame 0 - Left turning point

The bob hangs at its leftmost angle, +18 degrees from vertical.

- Velocity: 0 (instantaneous rest)
- Acceleration: maximum, pointing back toward center
- String drawn taut up and to the left of the pivot

```
   o      <- pivot
  /
 /
(@)       <- bob, far left
```

This is the start of the cycle. The next sampled position is
[[z=1|frame 1]], where the bob has begun to fall.

@plane z=1 x=-10 y=-6 label="Frame 1: falling left"
# Frame 1 - Descending from the left

Angle is now about +10 degrees. The bob has dropped and picked up speed.

- Velocity: rising (roughly half of peak)
- Acceleration: still inward, but smaller than at the extreme
- Notice the bob has moved closer to the bottom than frame 0 moved from rest

```
   o
   |\
   | \
   (@)
```

Gravity is doing positive work here. Continue to [[z=2|frame 2]] for the
fastest point of the swing.

@plane z=2 x=0 y=-9 label="Frame 2: bottom (max speed)"
# Frame 2 - Bottom of the arc

Angle is 0 degrees, the bob is directly below the pivot.

- Velocity: maximum of the whole swing
- Acceleration (tangential): 0, the string is vertical
- All of the height lost since [[z=0|frame 0]] is now kinetic energy

```
   o
   |
   |
   |
  (@)      <- straight down, moving fast
```

From here the bob climbs the right side and starts to slow. See
[[z=3|frame 3]].

@plane z=3 x=10 y=-6 label="Frame 3: rising right"
# Frame 3 - Climbing the right side

Angle is about -10 degrees. Mirror image of [[z=1|frame 1]], but moving
the other way.

- Velocity: falling (decelerating)
- Acceleration: inward again, now pointing back toward center from the right
- Kinetic energy is converting back into height

```
   o
  /|
 / |
   (@)
```

One more frame to the right extreme: [[z=4|frame 4]].

@plane z=4 x=18 y=0 label="Frame 4: right extreme"
# Frame 4 - Right turning point

Angle is -18 degrees, the mirror of [[z=0|frame 0]].

- Velocity: 0 (instantaneous rest)
- Acceleration: maximum, pointing back toward center
- Height matches the left extreme (energy conserved in the ideal case)

```
   o
    \
     \
     (@)   <- bob, far right
```

In a perfect pendulum this is the end of the forward pass. The loop
setting `ping-pong` now replays [[z=3|frame 3]], [[z=2|frame 2]],
[[z=1|frame 1]], and back to [[z=0|frame 0]].

@plane z=5 label="Notes: timing and damping"
# Animation notes

Not a drawn frame. This plane documents how to play the stack.

- **Order:** play z=0 through z=4, then reverse to z=0 for one full period.
- **Easing:** do not space frames evenly in time. The bob is slow at the
  extremes ([[z=0|frame 0]], [[z=4|frame 4]]) and fast at the bottom
  ([[z=2|frame 2]]), so ease-in-out on the angle reads as natural.
- **Damping:** for a decaying swing, shrink the x offsets a few percent
  each period so the extremes creep inward toward the pivot.
- **Frame rate:** at 12 fps a single pass takes under half a second; hold
  one extra tick on each turning point to sell the pause.

Jump back to the start at [[z=0|frame 0]].

title: Powers of Ten - a fall through every order of magnitude
src:
---
3md: 1.0
category: Information
axis: scale
view: single
title: Powers of Ten - a fall through every order of magnitude
subtitle: scrub Z to zoom from the cosmic web down to a single quark
---
The Z axis is magnification. Each plane is one big step inward, roughly an
order of magnitude smaller than the last. Start at the largest structure we
know and fall, scale by scale, all the way down to a speck inside a proton.
Scrub from [[z=0|the cosmic web]] to [[z=12|a quark]].

@plane z=0 label="10^27 m - the cosmic web"
# The Cosmic Web

The largest structure there is: galaxies strung along filaments of dark
matter, with vast empty voids between. From here the whole observable
universe is a single glittering froth.

```
 *   .      *        .
   \   .--*--.   /  *
 .--*-- the void --*--.
   /   *--.--*   \   .
 *     .      *      .
```

@plane z=1 label="10^21 m - a galaxy"
# The Milky Way

One bright knot on a filament resolves into a spiral: a hundred billion
suns wound around a quiet, hungry center. Our entire night sky is a single
arm of this pinwheel.

```
       . : * : .
   . *  \\___//  * .
  : * --( ***)-- * :
   . *  //‾‾‾\\  * .
       ' : * : '
```

@plane z=2 label="10^13 m - a star system"
# The Solar System

Zoom into one unremarkable star on one arm. Tiny worlds trace long ellipses
around it. Nearly all of this picture is empty space; the planets are
specks, the distances enormous.

```
        .         o
   o        ( ) ·    ·   o
        ·  ·    o
              ·
```

@plane z=3 label="10^9 m - a star"
# The Sun

The central speck swells into a roiling sphere of plasma, a million Earths
could fit inside. Its surface boils; loops of magnetic fire arc off the
edge and fall back.

```
    .-~~~~~-.
  /  ~ ~~ ~  \
 | ~~  ()  ~~ |
  \  ~~ ~ ~~ /
    '-~~~~~-'
```

@plane z=4 label="10^7 m - a planet"
# Earth

The third speck out. A blue marble wrapped in a skin of air so thin that,
at this scale, it would be a coat of varnish. Everything anyone has ever
done happened on this dot.

```
     _.-''-._
   .' o   ~~  '.
  / ~~   land   \
  | ocean   ~~  |
   \  ~~  land  /
    '._  ~~ _.'
```

@plane z=5 label="10^4 m - a city"
# A City At Dawn

Drop through the clouds: a grid of streets, rivers of early traffic,
ten thousand windows catching the first light. Each square is a life,
a kitchen, a kettle starting to whistle.

```
 ###  ##  ####  ##
 ## ::## ::## ::##
 ####  ##  ##  ####
 ## ::## ::##::  ##
```

@plane z=6 label="10^0 m - a person"
# One Meter

A single person on a single street, looking up. This is the scale we live
at, the only one that ever feels like home. Everything above is too big to
hold; everything below, too small to see. We balance here in the middle.

```
      O
     /|\
      |
     / \
```

@plane z=7 label="10^-4 m - skin"
# The Surface

Closer than eyes can focus. The smooth back of a hand becomes a landscape
of ridges and dead, flaking plates, dotted with the open mouths of pores.
You are, up close, mostly terrain.

```
~^~^~_~^~^~^~_~^~
^~ () ~^~ () ~^~^
~_~^~^~_~^~^~_~^~
```

@plane z=8 label="10^-5 m - a cell"
# A Living Cell

Into one pore, one cell. A wet, crowded city of its own: a nucleus at the
center holding the instructions, machinery churning in the cytoplasm
around it. Forty trillion of these are, right now, being you.

```
   .-‾‾‾‾‾‾-.
  /  . o .   \
 |  . (NUC) . |
  \   . o .  /
   '-______-'
```

@plane z=9 label="10^-8 m - DNA"
# The Double Helix

Inside the nucleus, the instructions themselves: two strands twisting
around each other, rungs of paired letters. Two meters of this is coiled
inside every cell, spelling the whole of you in four characters.

```
 A=T   .
  \   /
   T=A
  /   \
 C=G   '
  \   /
   G=C
```

@plane z=10 label="10^-10 m - an atom"
# A Single Atom

Down to one atom on one rung. A dense nucleus and a blur of electrons, but
mostly, overwhelmingly, empty space. If the nucleus were a marble, the
nearest electron would be a sand grain a kilometer away.

```
      . - .
    /   o   \
   |  (***)  |
    \   o   /
      ' - '
```

@plane z=11 label="10^-14 m - the nucleus"
# The Nucleus

Cross the empty kilometer to the marble. It splits into protons and
neutrons, packed tight and humming with the strongest force there is, the
one that holds everything heavier than hydrogen together.

```
   (p)(n)
  (n)(p)(n)
   (p)(n)
```

@plane z=12 label="10^-18 m - a quark"
# A Quark

Inside one proton, three quarks flicker in and out of being, bound by
glue we can never pull apart. This is the floor, or the deepest floor we
can see. From here, look back up: [[z=0|the cosmic web]] is only twelve
steps away.

```
    q
   / \
  q---q
```

title: Reef Tank Cross-Section
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: depth
title: Reef Tank Cross-Section
view: elevator
legend: C=🪸 F=🐟 S=🦈 T=🐠 A=🦀 U=🐙 O=🐚 G=🟩 B=🟦 W=🟫 .=·
tank: 120-gallon reef, 48"×24"×24"
category: Information
---

A living cross-section of a thriving reef aquarium — descend from the water surface to the sand bed.

@plane z=0 label="Surface Zone (0–4 in)"

## Surface Zone · *The Air–Water Interface*

```
B B B B B B B B B B B B
B . . F . . . . F . . B
B . . . . . . . . . . B
B . . . . T . . . . . B
B . . . . . . . . . . B
B B B B B B B B B B B B
```

**Inhabitants**
- **Clownfish** (Amphiprion ocellaris) — skimming just below the meniscus
- **Chromis** — schooling in open water

**Conditions**
| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Light | 350 µmol/m²/s PAR |
| Flow | High (return pump outlet) |
| Temp | 78 °F |

> Protein skimmer pulls surface film; auto top-off keeps salinity stable at 1.025.

@plane z=1 label="Mid-Water (4–12 in)"

## Mid-Water Column · *The Open Blue*

```
B . . . . . . . . . . B
B . . F . . . F . . . B
B . . . . . . . . F . B
B . F . . T . . . . . B
B . . . . . . F . . . B
B . . . . . . . . . . B
```

**Inhabitants**
- **Royal Gramma** — hovering near rockwork edges
- **Firefish** — darting into crevices when startled
- **Tangs** — grazing suspended algae particles

**Conditions**
| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Light | 200 µmol/m²/s PAR |
| Flow | Moderate (wave makers) |
| Salinity | 35 ppt |

Wave makers on opposite walls create a chaotic surge — mimicking natural reef energy.

@plane z=2 label="Coral Zone (12–18 in)"

## Coral Zone · *The Living Architecture*

```
B . C . . C C . . C . B
B C . C . . . C . . C B
B . C . C . C . C . . B
B C . . . C . . . C . B
B . C C . . C . C . C B
B C . . C . . C . . . B
```

**Coral Species**
- **SPS (Small Polyp Stony)** — Acropora, Montipora demanding high flow + light
- **LPS (Large Polyp Stony)** — Hammer, Torch, Frogspawn on shelf edges
- **Soft Corals** — Xenia pulsing rhythmically mid-column

**Conditions**
| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Light | 120–180 µmol/m²/s PAR |
| Alkalinity | 8.5 dKH |
| Calcium | 440 ppm |
| Magnesium | 1350 ppm |

> Dosing two-part (Ca + Alk) maintains stability. Test twice weekly.

**Coral Placement Logic**
- [ ] SPS anchored to highest rock peaks
- [ ] LPS on mid-level ledges (avoid flow direct hits)
- [ ] Softies on lower sides, away from stinging LPS

@plane z=3 label="Rockwork (18–22 in)"

## Rockwork · *The Skeletal Reef*

```
W . W W . . W W . . W W
W W . W W . W . W . W .
. W W . W W . W W W . W
W . W . . W W . . W W .
W W . W . W . W . . W W
. W W W W . W W W W . W
```

**What Lives in the Rock**
- **Emerald Crabs** — cleaning algae from crevices
- **Pistol Shrimp** — snapping in unseen tunnels
- **Feather Dusters** — crown extended, filtering plankton
- **Nassarius Snails** — emerging at feeding time

**Live Rock Facts**
- 80 lbs of aquacultured Reef Saver rock (lightweight, porous)
- 3–5 year established biofilm — the invisible engine of the nitrogen cycle
- Coralline algae (purple/pink crust) signals excellent water quality

**Nitrogen Cycle — [[z=4|see sand bed]] for final stage**

```
Ammonia → Nitrite → Nitrate → (exported via refugium / water change)
  (fish)   (Nitrosomonas) (Nitrobacter)
```

@plane z=4 label="Sand Bed (22–24 in)"

## Sand Bed · *The Living Floor*

```
A . . O . . A . O . . A
. O A . . O . . . A O .
O . . A O . A . O . . O
. A O . . A . O A . O .
A . . O A . . O . A . O
W W W W W W W W W W W W
```

**Sand Bed Crew**
- **Nassarius Snails** — burrowing, aerating, devouring detritus
- **Fighting Conch** — plowing furrows through the sand surface
- **Serpent Starfish** — arms extending under rocks at night
- **Sand-Sifting Cucumbers** — processing a tablespoon of sand per hour

**Substrate Details**
| Detail | Spec |
|--------|------|
| Depth | 1 inch (shallow — avoids anaerobic dead zones) |
| Grain size | 0.5–1.0 mm aragonite |
| Composition | CaribSea Arag-Alive |
| Function | Biological filtration + calcium buffer |

> A shallow sand bed (< 2 in) stays oxygenated throughout. Deep beds (> 4 in) risk hydrogen sulfide pockets — avoid unless you are running a specialized DSB refugium.

**The bottom of the tank is the beginning of the cycle.** Detritus settles here, is processed by the clean-up crew, and the byproducts feed the corals above — back to [[z=2|the coral zone]].

title: Reentry Blackout Window
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: minute
title: Reentry Blackout Window
view: stack
fps: 2
category: Engineering
---

A spacecraft reentry told as a stack of six minutes. The Z axis is time, but the
planes also move through heat, signal loss, and recovery.

@plane z=0 label="T minus 02"
# T minus 02

```
        EARTH LIMB
  ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

             capsule
                v
             [###]
              / \

altitude: 122 km
speed:    7.7 km/s
link:     green
```

- Guidance trims angle of attack to 6.4 degrees.
- Cabin hears only fans, suit radios, and checklist cadence.
- Plasma is not here yet. The air ahead is still mostly vacuum.

Next: [[z=1|interface]].

@plane z=1 label="Interface"
# Entry Interface

```
  density: first bite

             [###]
            /|||||\
           / ||||| \
          /  |||||  \

heat rate: rising
g load:    0.8
link:      green with noise
```

The vehicle is not flying through air so much as spending speed into it.

| Channel | Reading |
|---|---|
| Inertial | locked |
| Drag accel | climbing |
| Heat shield | warming evenly |

@plane z=2 label="Glow"
# Orange Sheath

```
             . . . .
          .  :::::  .
        .   :[###]:   .
          .  :::::  .
             ' ' ' '

heat rate:  peak soon
g load:     2.6
link:       packet loss
```

Ionized air wraps the capsule. The antennas are still talking, but every packet
has to fight its way out.

- Forward window: opaque orange
- Roll rate: damped
- Crew callout: "holding attitude"

@plane z=3 label="Blackout"
# Blackout

```
████████████████████████
█                      █
█       NO CARRIER      █
█                      █
████████████████████████

heat rate:  peak
g load:     4.1
link:       lost
```

Ground sees telemetry become silence. Nothing is wrong yet. This is the planned
quiet part, which somehow makes it harder to watch.

Checklist:

- [x] Attitude stable before loss
- [x] Corridor confirmed
- [ ] Wait for acquisition

@plane z=4 label="Acquisition"
# Acquisition of Signal

```
       carrier lock
            |
            v
        [ . . . ]
       [  ###  ]
        [ . . . ]

heat rate: falling
g load:    2.2
link:      weak but real
```

The first bit of signal returns as a thin line in the noise. Then voice.

> "We read you."

The room exhales in pieces.

@plane z=5 label="Chute Armed"
# Chute Armed

```
        drogue box: armed
        main box:   inhibited

             [###]
              | |
              | |

altitude: 24 km
speed:    subsonic soon
link:     green
```

Reentry is no longer a flame problem. It is a sequence problem.

- Drogues at target dynamic pressure.
- Mains only after rate decay.
- Recovery beacon remains quiet until splashdown.

Back to [[z=0|the top of the corridor]].

title: Seed Germination: Frame-by-Frame
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: frame
title: Seed Germination: Frame-by-Frame
view: play
fps: 4
species: Phaseolus vulgaris (common bean)
days_elapsed: 0–10 DAP (days after planting)
depth_cm: 5
category: Animation
---
*Phaseolus vulgaris* — watch hypocotyl emergence through soil break, rendered at 4 fps.

@plane z=0 label="Day 0 — Dry Seed"
```
  soil surface
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
                                
  [ seed ]                      
   (  )                         
   ~~~~                         
                                
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
```
**Day 0 — Imbibition begins.**
Seed coat absorbs ~150% its dry weight in water within 6 h. Metabolic rate: near zero. Axis embryo still compressed. No radicle visible.

@plane z=1 label="Day 2 — Radicle Emerges"
```
  soil surface
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
                                
  [ seed ]                      
   (  )                         
     |   ← radicle              
                                
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
```
**Day 2 — Radicle protrusion.**
Primary root punches through micropyle end. Gravitropism kicks in; auxin redistributes to lower flank. Root cap secretes mucilage to lubricate soil passage.

@plane z=2 label="Day 3 — Hypocotyl Hook"
```
  soil surface
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
                                
   ( )                          
    |                           
    |  ← hypocotyl elongating   
    |                           
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
```
**Day 3 — Hypocotyl straightens upward.**
Hook unfolding driven by differential ethylene on concave side. Root hairs begin forming 2–4 mm behind root tip. Stored cotyledon starch mobilising via amylase.

@plane z=3 label="Day 5 — Soil Break"
```
  soil surface
  ┌─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─┐
  ↑ breaking through           
   \                           
    | ← hypocotyl              
    |                           
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
```
**Day 5 — Emergence (VE stage).**
Apical hook breaches surface. Cotyledons still folded, protecting the shoot apical meristem. Chloroplast differentiation begins on light exposure. The seedling is now autotrophic-adjacent.

@plane z=4 label="Day 7 — Cotyledons Open"
```
 \\   //  ← cotyledons spread  
  \\ //                        
   \\/                         
    |                          
    |                          
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
```
**Day 7 — Cotyledons unfurl.**
Chlorophyll a/b ratio ~2.8. Stomata open; transpiration begins. First photosynthetic CO₂ fixation detectable. Epicotyl elongating between cotyledon axils toward first true leaves.

@plane z=5 label="Day 10 — First True Leaves"
```
   _/ \_   ← true leaf 1 (V1)  
  /     \                      
 \\     //  ← cotyledons       
   \\ //                       
    ||                         
    ||                         
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
```
**Day 10 — V1 stage.**
Unifoliate primary leaves fully expanded (~4 cm²). Cotyledons yellowing as reserves depleted. Trifoliate leaves initiating in SAM. Root system: taproot 8 cm + lateral network forming. Seedling is fully established.

title: Solar System by Depth
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: depth
title: Solar System by Depth
view: elevator
unit: AU
scale: logarithmic
source: JPL Horizons, IAU 2006
category: Information
---
Descend outward from the Sun. Each plane is a world — real distances, real textures.

@plane z=0 label="☀ Sun · 0 AU"
# The Sun

**Diameter:** 1,392,700 km — 109 Earths side by side  
**Surface temp:** 5,778 K (photosphere) · 15,000,000 K (core)  
**Composition:** 74% H, 24% He, 2% heavier elements  
**Rotation:** 25 days at equator, 36 days at poles — differential rotation

> The Sun contains **99.86%** of all mass in the solar system. Everything else is rounding error.

The solar wind streams outward at 400–800 km/s, carving the heliosphere — a bubble 120 AU across that shields us from interstellar cosmic rays.

**Light travel time from here:** 0 seconds, obviously.

@plane z=1 label="☿ Mercury · 0.39 AU"
# Mercury · 0.39 AU

**Diameter:** 4,879 km  
**Orbital period:** 87.97 Earth days  
**Day length:** 58.65 Earth days — 3:2 spin-orbit resonance  
**Surface temp:** −180 °C (night) to +430 °C (day)

Mercury has almost **no atmosphere** — exospheric pressure ~10⁻¹⁵ bar. Without a blanket, heat escapes instantly into space each night.

Its enormous iron core is ~85% of its radius — proportionally the largest in the solar system. One hypothesis: a giant impact stripped the mantle early on.

**Light travel time from Sun:** 3 min 13 sec at perihelion

- [ ] MESSENGER orbited 2011–2015, mapping 100% of surface
- [ ] BepiColombo en route, arrival 2025

@plane z=2 label="♀ Venus · 0.72 AU"
# Venus · 0.72 AU

**Diameter:** 12,104 km  
**Orbital period:** 224.7 days  
**Day length:** 243 Earth days — *longer than its year*  
**Surface temp:** 465 °C (mean) — hotter than Mercury

Venus rotates **retrograde** — the Sun rises in the west. No one knows why for certain. A massive ancient impact is the leading candidate.

The atmosphere is 96.5% CO₂ at 92 bar — the pressure of 900 m underwater on Earth. Lead would melt on the surface.

> Clouds of **sulfuric acid** float at 45–70 km altitude, permanently hiding the surface from visible light. Radar sees through them.

**Light travel time from Sun:** 6 min at mean distance  
**Closest approach to Earth:** 38.2 million km — our nearest planetary neighbor

@plane z=3 label="♁ Earth · 1.00 AU"
# Earth · 1.00 AU

**Diameter:** 12,742 km  
**Orbital period:** 365.25 days  
**Axial tilt:** 23.44° (gives us seasons)  
**Surface temp:** −88 °C to +58 °C (mean: +15 °C)

The **1 AU** standard: 149,597,870.7 km, defined by the distance light travels in 499.0 seconds.

One moon. Unusually large — mass ratio 1:81 vs Earth. The Moon stabilizes Earth's axial tilt to within ~2.4°, making our climate predictable over geological time. Mars, with two tiny moons, wobbles 0–60° over millions of years.

**Water:** 71% of surface. Liquid water requires the right pressure *and* temperature — only possible in the habitable zone. We're in it by ~500 km margin on the inner edge.

- **Magnetic field:** 25–65 µT, generated by a liquid iron outer core
- **Atmosphere:** 78% N₂, 21% O₂, 1% Ar — the O₂ is entirely biological

@plane z=4 label="♂ Mars · 1.52 AU"
# Mars · 1.52 AU

**Diameter:** 6,779 km — half of Earth's  
**Orbital period:** 686.97 days  
**Axial tilt:** 25.19° — nearly Earth-like  
**Surface temp:** −153 °C to +20 °C (mean: −63 °C)

Olympus Mons rises **21.9 km** above the mean datum — the tallest volcano in the solar system. Valles Marineris stretches 4,000 km, 7 km deep — the Grand Canyon is a crack in a sidewalk by comparison.

Mars lost its magnetic field ~4 billion years ago. Without it, the solar wind stripped the atmosphere. Today: 0.6% of Earth's sea-level pressure, 95.3% CO₂.

> **Did Mars have liquid water?** Yes — river deltas, lake beds, and hydrated minerals confirm it persisted for ~1 billion years early in Martian history.

**Moons:** Phobos (27 km) and Deimos (15 km) — captured asteroids, or debris from a giant impact. Phobos orbits in 7 h 39 min — faster than Mars rotates. It will collide or break apart in ~50 million years.

@plane z=5 label="⚶ Asteroid Belt · 2.2–3.2 AU"
# Asteroid Belt · 2.2–3.2 AU

**Total mass:** ~4% of the Moon — less than you'd think  
**Largest body:** Ceres, 940 km, a dwarf planet  
**Object count:** ~1.1–1.9 million bodies > 1 km

The belt is **not dense**. Every spacecraft that has flown through it passed without incident. Average spacing between kilometer-class rocks: ~1 million km.

Jupiter's gravity prevents this material from accreting into a planet — orbital resonances (Kirkwood gaps at 2.50, 2.82, 2.95 AU) eject asteroids into planet-crossing orbits.

**Composition gradient:**
- Inner belt: silicate S-type (rocky, dry)
- Outer belt: carbonaceous C-type (dark, water-rich, primordial)

> C-type asteroids are chemically similar to the Sun minus volatiles — nearly unchanged since 4.56 billion years ago.

Ceres has water ice beneath its surface and **bright salt deposits** (sodium carbonates) in Occator Crater — remnants of an ancient briny ocean.

@plane z=6 label="♃ Jupiter · 5.20 AU"
# Jupiter · 5.20 AU

**Diameter:** 139,820 km — 11 Earths wide  
**Mass:** 318 × Earth — more than all other planets combined × 2  
**Orbital period:** 11.86 years  
**Day length:** 9 h 56 min — fastest rotation in the solar system

The **Great Red Spot** is a storm 1.3× Earth's diameter that has persisted for at least 350 years. It has been shrinking since the 1800s; current size: ~16,000 km wide.

Jupiter is a **failed star** in the sense that it is mostly H and He, but it would need ~80× more mass to ignite hydrogen fusion. It does radiate 1.67× more heat than it receives from the Sun — residual heat from gravitational contraction.

**Moons:** 95 known. The Galilean four are worlds in their own right:
- **Io:** most volcanically active body in the solar system (tidal heating)
- **Europa:** 10–30 km ice shell over a liquid water ocean — prime astrobiology target
- **Ganymede:** larger than Mercury, has its own magnetosphere
- **Callisto:** ancient, heavily cratered, barely geologically active

**Light travel time from Sun:** 43 min 16 sec

@plane z=7 label="♄ Saturn · 9.58 AU"
# Saturn · 9.58 AU

**Diameter:** 116,460 km (equatorial) — oblate due to fast spin  
**Bulk density:** 0.687 g/cm³ — less than water  
**Orbital period:** 29.46 years  
**Day length:** 10 h 33 min

The rings span **282,000 km** in diameter but are on average only **~10 meters thick** — the thinnest structure relative to its width of any object known. If scaled to a sheet of paper (0.1 mm), the rings would be 2.8 km wide.

Ring material is **93% water ice**, with trace silicates. The rings are geologically young — likely 10–100 million years old, formed when a moon wandered too close and broke apart.

> Cassini (1997–2017) revealed that ring material rains onto Saturn at ~10,000 kg/sec. The rings may be gone in 100 million years.

**Titan:** 5,150 km diameter. The only moon with a dense atmosphere (1.5 bar, mostly N₂). Lakes and rivers of liquid methane and ethane pool near the poles. Surface pressure and temperature mimic early Earth — in a deep-freeze analog.

**Light travel time from Sun:** 1 h 20 min at mean distance

title: The Solar System by Distance
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: space
title: The Solar System by Distance
subtitle: Eight planets, ordered outward from the Sun
units: astronomical units (1 AU is the Earth-Sun distance)
author: CorvidLabs Gallery
---
A walk outward from the Sun. Each plane sits at the planet's mean orbital
distance, mapped so that `z` equals the distance in astronomical units (AU).
The inner rocky worlds are packed close together near `z=1`, while the outer
gas and ice giants spread out across tens of AU. Start at [[z=0.39|Mercury]]
and travel to the edge at [[z=30.07|Neptune]].

@plane z=0.39 x=0 y=0 label="Mercury"
# Mercury (0.39 AU)

The smallest planet and the closest to the Sun. Mercury races around its orbit
in just 88 Earth days, the fastest year in the system.

- **Type:** rocky (terrestrial)
- **Day length:** 59 Earth days (a single rotation)
- **Surface:** scorched by day, frozen by night, swinging from +430 C to -180 C
- **Moons:** none

No atmosphere worth the name means no blanket to trap heat, so the temperature
swing between the sunlit and shadowed sides is the most extreme of any planet.

Next stop outward: [[z=0.72|Venus]].

@plane z=0.72 x=0.33 y=0 label="Venus"
# Venus (0.72 AU)

Earth's near-twin in size, and the hottest planet in the system. A thick
carbon-dioxide atmosphere traps heat so efficiently that the surface sits at
about 465 C, hotter than Mercury despite being farther from the Sun.

- **Type:** rocky (terrestrial)
- **Atmosphere:** 96 percent CO2, surface pressure about 92 times Earth's
- **Spin:** retrograde (the Sun rises in the west) and very slow
- **Moons:** none

A day on Venus (243 Earth days) is longer than its year (225 Earth days).

Inward to [[z=0.39|Mercury]], or onward to [[z=1.0|Earth]].

@plane z=1.0 x=0.66 y=0 label="Earth"
# Earth (1.00 AU)

The reference point. One AU is defined by Earth's average distance from the Sun,
about 150 million kilometers, which is why this plane sits at exactly `z=1`.

- **Type:** rocky (terrestrial)
- **Atmosphere:** 78 percent nitrogen, 21 percent oxygen
- **Surface:** the only known world with stable liquid water and life
- **Moons:** 1 (the Moon, at roughly 384,000 km)

Sunlight takes about 8 minutes and 20 seconds to reach here.

The next world out is the red one: [[z=1.52|Mars]].

@plane z=1.52 x=1.0 y=0 label="Mars"
# Mars (1.52 AU)

The red planet, colored by iron oxide dust. Mars is half Earth's diameter and
hosts the tallest volcano in the system, Olympus Mons, rising about 22 km.

- **Type:** rocky (terrestrial)
- **Day length:** 24 hours 37 minutes (close to Earth's)
- **Atmosphere:** thin CO2, about 0.6 percent of Earth's surface pressure
- **Moons:** 2 (Phobos and Deimos, both small and lumpy)

Beyond Mars lies the asteroid belt, the boundary between the rocky inner system
and the giants. Cross it to reach [[z=5.2|Jupiter]].

@plane z=5.2 x=2.5 y=0 label="Jupiter"
# Jupiter (5.20 AU)

The giant. Jupiter holds more than twice the mass of every other planet
combined, and its Great Red Spot is a storm wide enough to swallow Earth.

- **Type:** gas giant
- **Day length:** under 10 hours (the fastest spin in the system)
- **Composition:** mostly hydrogen and helium, no solid surface
- **Moons:** 95 confirmed, including the four large Galilean moons (Io, Europa,
  Ganymede, Callisto)

Note the jump in scale: Jupiter sits more than three times farther out than
Mars, and the spacing only grows from here.

Onward to the ringed world: [[z=9.58|Saturn]].

@plane z=9.58 x=4.5 y=0 label="Saturn"
# Saturn (9.58 AU)

Famous for its rings, a vast disk of ice and rock chunks only tens of meters
thick but spanning hundreds of thousands of kilometers. Saturn is the least
dense planet, lighter than water on average.

- **Type:** gas giant
- **Day length:** about 10.7 hours
- **Rings:** seven main groups, mostly water ice
- **Moons:** 146 confirmed, including Titan (thicker atmosphere than Earth's)

From here the system turns cold and blue. Continue to [[z=19.22|Uranus]].

@plane z=19.22 x=8 y=0 label="Uranus"
# Uranus (19.22 AU)

The tilted ice giant. Uranus rotates almost on its side, tipped about 98
degrees, so each pole spends roughly 42 years in sunlight and 42 in darkness.

- **Type:** ice giant
- **Color:** pale cyan from methane in the upper atmosphere
- **Day length:** about 17 hours, retrograde
- **Moons:** 28 confirmed, named for characters from Shakespeare and Pope

Nearly twice as far out as Saturn. One step remains to the edge: [[z=30.07|Neptune]].

@plane z=30.07 x=12 y=0 label="Neptune"
# Neptune (30.07 AU)

The outermost planet, a deep-blue ice giant with the fastest winds in the
system, reaching about 2,000 km/h. Sunlight here is roughly 900 times fainter
than on Earth, and a single Neptune year lasts about 165 Earth years.

- **Type:** ice giant
- **Discovery:** found in 1846 by mathematical prediction before it was seen
- **Day length:** about 16 hours
- **Moons:** 16 confirmed, including Triton, which orbits backward

This is the end of the planetary road. Beyond lies the Kuiper Belt, home to
Pluto and other dwarf planets.

Jump back to the start at [[z=1.0|Earth]] or all the way in to [[z=0.39|Mercury]].

title: Ser vs. Estar: The One That Always Trips You Up
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: side
title: Ser vs. Estar: The One That Always Trips You Up
view: single
deck: Spanish Flashcards
level: A2–B1
author: Leif
category: Education
---
Flip through 6 cards. Each card has a **front** (z=even) and a **back** (z=odd). Try to answer before you flip. ([[z=1|→ flip card 1]])

@plane z=0 label="Card 1 — Front"
## ¿Cómo **___ ** tú hoy?

*Fill in the blank: ser or estar?*

---

> Hint: think about whether "today" makes it temporary or permanent.

[[z=1|→ flip to see answer]]

@plane z=1 label="Card 1 — Back"
## ¿Cómo **estás** tú hoy?

✅ **estar** — because mood and health are *temporary states*

---

**The rule:** ESTAR covers **H**ow you feel, **A**ppearance right now, **L**ocation, **T**emporary conditions — remember **HALT**.

> *"Estoy cansado."* — I'm tired (right now, not forever)
> *"Estoy en Madrid."* — I'm in Madrid (location)

[[z=2|→ next card]]

@plane z=2 label="Card 2 — Front"
## Ella **___ ** médica.

*Fill in the blank: ser or estar?*

---

> Hint: is being a doctor a temporary condition or a defining identity?

[[z=3|→ flip to see answer]]

@plane z=3 label="Card 2 — Back"
## Ella **es** médica.

✅ **ser** — profession is a *defining characteristic*

---

**The rule:** SER covers **D**escriptions, **O**rigin, **C**haracteristics, **T**ime, **O**ccupation, **R**elationships — remember **DOCTOR**.

> *"Soy de Seattle."* — I'm from Seattle (origin)
> *"Son las tres."* — It's three o'clock (time)

[[z=4|→ next card]]

@plane z=4 label="Card 3 — Front"
## La puerta **___ ** abierta.

*Fill in the blank: ser or estar?*

---

> Hint: *abierta* (open) — is this a characteristic of the door, or its current condition?

[[z=5|→ flip to see answer]]

@plane z=5 label="Card 3 — Back"
## La puerta **está** abierta.

✅ **estar** — the door is *currently open*, not permanently open by nature

---

**The trap:** Many past participles change meaning with ser vs. estar:

| | ser | estar |
|---|---|---|
| aburrido | boring (personality) | bored (right now) |
| listo | clever | ready |
| malo | evil | sick |
| rico | wealthy | delicious |

> *"El café **es** rico."* — Coffee is delicious (characteristic)
> *"Estoy listo."* — I'm ready (state)

[[z=6|→ next card]]

@plane z=6 label="Card 4 — Front"
## La boda **___ ** en la iglesia.

*Fill in the blank: ser or estar?*

---

> Hint: events have locations — but which verb do you use for an *event's* location vs. a *thing's* location?

[[z=7|→ flip to see answer]]

@plane z=7 label="Card 4 — Back"
## La boda **es** en la iglesia.

✅ **ser** — events use *ser* for location, not estar!

---

**The exception that breaks your brain:**

- *Things and people* use **estar** for location → *El libro **está** en la mesa.*
- *Events* use **ser** for location → *La fiesta **es** aquí.*

> Think of it this way: events *happen at* a place (defining attribute), while objects are merely *found at* a place (temporary).

[[z=8|→ next card]]

@plane z=8 label="Card 5 — Front"
## El cielo **___ ** azul.

*Fill in the blank: ser or estar?*

---

> Hint: is the sky being blue a permanent truth or today's weather?

[[z=9|→ flip to see answer]]

@plane z=9 label="Card 5 — Back"
## El cielo **es** azul.

✅ **ser** — the sky is *inherently, characteristically* blue

---

**But wait** — context can flip it:

> *"Hoy el cielo **está** muy azul."*
> Today the sky is very blue. (unusually vivid — a temporary observation)

This is the advanced nuance: both can be grammatically valid, but they express **different meanings**. Ser = inherent truth. Estar = the speaker's observation in this moment.

[[z=10|→ final card]]

@plane z=10 label="Card 6 — Front"
## Translate: *"She used to be happy, but now she's sad."*

*Use the correct form of ser or estar for each clause.*

---

> Hint: happiness — is it a personality trait or a temporary state? Does your answer change the meaning?

[[z=11|→ flip to see answer]]

@plane z=11 label="Card 6 — Back"
## Two valid answers, two meanings:

**"Ella **era** feliz, pero ahora **está** triste."**
→ She *used to be* a happy person by nature, but now she's (temporarily) sad.

**"Ella **estaba** feliz, pero ahora **está** triste."**
→ She was feeling happy earlier, but now she's feeling sad.

---

> The first sentence implies a *personality change* — something significant happened.
> The second is just mood shifting through the day.

**You've finished the deck.** 🎉

- [[z=0|↩ start over]] from card 1
- Or grab a notebook: write 3 sentences today using *estar* for a temporary feeling, and check yourself tomorrow.

title: 12-Week Strength Mesocycle (4-day)
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: week
view: single
title: 12-Week Strength Mesocycle (4-day)
category: Health
lifts: squat / bench / deadlift / press
deloads: weeks 4, 8, 12
---
A periodized program. Each plane is one training week; scrub Z to move through
the block. Three phases of four weeks: accumulation, intensification, peaking,
each capped by a [[z=3|deload]]. Loads are %1RM, effort is RPE.

@plane z=0 label="Wk 1 - Accumulation"
**Accumulation 1/3** - build volume, RPE 7

- Mon - Squat 4x8 @70% · RDL 3x10
- Tue - Bench 4x8 @70% · Row 4x10
- Thu - Front Squat 3x8 · Leg curl 3x12
- Fri - OHP 4x8 @68% · Pull-up 4x8

@plane z=1 label="Wk 2 - Accumulation"
**Accumulation 2/3** - more sets, RPE 8

- Mon - Squat 5x8 @72% · RDL 4x10
- Tue - Bench 5x8 @72% · Row 4x10
- Thu - Front Squat 4x8 · Leg curl 4x12
- Fri - OHP 5x8 @70% · Pull-up 4x10

@plane z=2 label="Wk 3 - Accumulation"
**Accumulation 3/3** - peak volume, RPE 9

- Mon - Squat 5x10 @72% · RDL 4x12
- Tue - Bench 5x10 @72% · Row 5x10
- Thu - Front Squat 4x10 · Leg curl 4x15
- Fri - OHP 5x10 @70% · Pull-up 5x10

@plane z=3 label="Wk 4 - DELOAD"
**Deload** - cut volume ~50%, RPE 5. Recover.

- Mon - Squat 3x5 @60%
- Tue - Bench 3x5 @60%
- Thu - light technique work
- Fri - OHP 3x5 @58%

Next: [[z=4|intensification block]].

@plane z=4 label="Wk 5 - Intensification"
**Intensification 1/3** - load up, RPE 7

- Mon - Squat 5x5 @78% · RDL 3x8
- Tue - Bench 5x5 @78% · Row 4x8
- Thu - Pause Squat 4x5 · Leg curl 3x10
- Fri - OHP 5x5 @76% · Pull-up 4x6

@plane z=5 label="Wk 6 - Intensification"
**Intensification 2/3** - heavier, RPE 8

- Mon - Squat 5x4 @82% · RDL 3x6
- Tue - Bench 5x4 @82% · Row 4x6
- Thu - Pause Squat 4x4 · Leg curl 3x10
- Fri - OHP 5x4 @80% · Weighted pull-up 4x5

@plane z=6 label="Wk 7 - Intensification"
**Intensification 3/3** - top loads, RPE 9

- Mon - Squat 4x3 @86% · RDL 3x5
- Tue - Bench 4x3 @86% · Row 4x6
- Thu - Pause Squat 3x3 · Leg curl 3x10
- Fri - OHP 4x3 @84% · Weighted pull-up 4x4

@plane z=7 label="Wk 8 - DELOAD"
**Deload** - cut intensity, RPE 5. Prime for the peak.

- Mon - Squat 3x4 @65%
- Tue - Bench 3x4 @65%
- Thu - light technique work
- Fri - OHP 3x4 @63%

Next: [[z=8|peaking block]].

@plane z=8 label="Wk 9 - Peaking"
**Peaking 1/3** - heavy triples, RPE 8

- Mon - Squat 4x3 @88% · light RDL 2x6
- Tue - Bench 4x3 @88% · Row 3x6
- Thu - Deadlift 4x3 @85%
- Fri - OHP 4x3 @86%

@plane z=9 label="Wk 10 - Peaking"
**Peaking 2/3** - doubles, RPE 9

- Mon - Squat 3x2 @91%
- Tue - Bench 3x2 @91% · Row 3x6
- Thu - Deadlift 3x2 @89%
- Fri - OHP 3x2 @89%

@plane z=10 label="Wk 11 - Peaking"
**Peaking 3/3** - openers, sharp and short

- Mon - Squat singles @93-95%
- Tue - Bench singles @93-95%
- Thu - Deadlift singles @92-94%
- Fri - rest / mobility

@plane z=11 label="Wk 12 - TEST / DELOAD"
**Test week** - work up to a new 1RM, then back off.

- Mon - Squat: work to a daily max
- Wed - Bench: work to a daily max
- Fri - Deadlift: work to a daily max

Then rest a week and restart at [[z=0|accumulation]] with the new maxes.

title: Supply Chain Port Map - Coffee Lot 42
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: layer
title: Supply Chain Port Map - Coffee Lot 42
view: stack
category: Maps
---

A logistics map for one coffee lot. Each layer adds a different part of the
route, from origin to exception handling.

@plane z=0 label="Origin"
# Origin Layer

```
FARM CLUSTER

 A1  A2  A3
  \  |  /
   mill-7
     |
  dry warehouse
```

- Lot: 42
- Origin: Huehuetenango
- Bags: 160
- Moisture: 10.8 percent

@plane z=1 label="Inland"
# Inland Trucking

```
dry warehouse
     |
     v  214 km
customs pre-check
     |
     v   38 km
Puerto Barrios gate
```

Two checkpoints matter: customs documents and container seal. The map keeps
both visible before the ship layer.

@plane z=2 label="Ocean"
# Ocean Leg

```
Puerto Barrios
      |
      |  vessel MV North Star
      v
Houston berth 12

ETA: 2026-07-18
temperature logger: active
```

The ocean layer tracks vessel, berth, ETA, and logger status without hiding the
origin story.

@plane z=3 label="Exceptions"
# Exception Layer

```
risk board

[ ] seal mismatch
[x] rain delay at gate
[ ] temp excursion
[ ] document hold
```

Rain delayed gate entry by six hours. No quality risk, but downstream roasting
slots should move by one day.

@plane z=4 label="Arrival"
# Arrival Plan

```
Houston berth 12
     |
     v
bonded warehouse
     |
     +--> QC sample
     |
     +--> roaster slot B
```

The final layer turns the route into actions: inspect, sample, schedule, roast.

title: Suspension Bridge: Load Path in Layers
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: layer
title: Suspension Bridge: Load Path in Layers
view: stack
legend: T=🟫 C=🟦 S=🟥 D=🟨 .=⬛ ^=🔼 |=⬜ ~=🌊
category: Engineering
---

A suspension bridge doesn't just hold itself up — it *redirects* forces through a chain of clever handoffs. Each layer here peels back one structural system, revealing how a truck's weight travels from deck to earth.

@plane z=0 label="Full Bridge"

```
..........T.....T.....T.....T..........
.........T|T...T|T...T|T...T|T.........
........T.|.T.T.|.T.T.|.T.T.|.T........
.......T..|..T..|..T..|..T..|..T.......
......T...|...T.|..T..|.T...|...T......
.....CCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCC....
.....|..............D...............|...
SSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSS
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
```

**The complete picture.** A loaded span carries a truck (D) across the stiffening girder deck. Above it, cables (C) sweep catenary curves between two towers (T). Below, the roadway sits on vertical hangers (|). The whole system bears on concrete anchor blocks and piers (S) at the shores.

> Every force you see here is either **tension** (pulling) or **compression** (pushing) — nothing else.

@plane z=1 label="Dead + Live Load"

```
..............🚛..............
......................................
    ↓ 800 kN     ↓ 800 kN
- - - - - D - - - D - - - - -
    ↓           ↓
```

**Where the load enters.** The truck applies concentrated **dead load** (its own weight, constant) plus **live load** (moving, variable) directly to the **stiffening girder** — the horizontal beam running the length of the deck.

- Dead load: self-weight of deck, girder, hangers, cables
- Live load: vehicles, pedestrians, wind, snow
- The girder's job: **distribute** point loads into many smaller forces along its length

**Key principle:** The girder resists local bending, preventing the cable from deforming into an ugly kink at every truck wheel.

@plane z=2 label="Hangers: Tension Transfer"

```
    |       |       |       |
    |  ↑T   |  ↑T   |  ↑T   |
    |       |       |       |
====|=======|=======|=======|====  ← stiffening girder
    ↑       ↑       ↑       ↑
  (load panels transferred upward)
```

**Hangers carry tension — only tension.** Vertical steel rods (or wire bundles) hang from the main cable above and attach to the girder below.

- Each panel of deck load is **lifted** by its hanger
- Hangers cannot push — they go slack if compressed
- Typical hanger spacing: **6 – 12 m**
- Material: high-strength galvanized wire rope or parallel wire strands

> *If a hanger snapped, its load would redistribute to adjacent hangers — the bridge would sag locally but not collapse.*

@plane z=3 label="Main Cable: Catenary Curve"

```
T                               T
 \                             /
  \           C C C           /
   \       C       C       /
    \   C               C   /
     \ /                 \ /
      *                   *
   (low point)         (low point)
      ↓ max tension here ↓
```

**The cable chooses its own shape.** Under uniform load a freely hanging cable forms a **parabola** (not a catenary — that's self-weight only). The main cable is a bundle of thousands of parallel high-strength wires, **cold-drawn** to 1,570 MPa tensile strength.

- Sag ratio (sag ÷ span) is typically **1:10**
- Shallower sag → higher cable tension, larger anchor forces
- Steeper sag → lower tension, but more material and taller towers

The cable is **always in pure tension** — it cannot carry bending. This is why suspension bridges can span so far: tension members are extremely efficient.

@plane z=4 label="Towers: Compression Path"

```
      ████
      ████   ← cable saddle at crown
      ████
     ██████
     ██████
    ████████
    ████████
   ██████████
   ██████████
  ████████████
  ████████████  ← base: max compression
SSSSSSSSSSSSSSSS
```

**Towers carry compression — straight down.** The cable pulls inward and downward on each tower top (the **saddle**). The tower converts this diagonal cable force into a **near-vertical compressive load** transmitted to its foundation.

- Tower material: **steel box sections** (modern) or **reinforced concrete**
- Golden Gate towers: 227 m tall, weigh 22,000 tonnes each
- Base pressure on bedrock: up to **30 MPa**

> *Towers are the only major compression members in a suspension bridge — everything else is in tension.*

@plane z=5 label="Anchor Blocks: Force Resolution"

```
cable →→→→→→→→→→→\
                   \  resultant
                    \  ↘
 ════════════════════ ANCHOR BLOCK ════
                    ↙
 uplift ↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑ weight of concrete resists
 
 ████████████████████████████████████
 ████  GRAVITY ANCHOR  ██████████████   ~100,000 tonnes
 ████████████████████████████████████
 ||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||
         bedrock / soil
```

**The anchor block closes the load path.** Cables pull outward and upward on each anchor. A massive concrete block (gravity anchor) or a tunnel drilled into rock (rock anchor) provides the equal-and-opposite reaction.

- Gravity anchor: pure dead weight resists cable pull
- Rock anchor: cable strands fan out and bond into drilled holes
- The horizontal cable component → **anchor must not slide**
- The vertical cable component → **anchor must not tip**

**Load path complete:**
`Truck → Girder → Hangers → Cable → Towers → Foundations → Earth`
`       (bending) (tension) (tension) (compression)     (bearing)`

@plane z=6 label="Force Magnitude Map"

| Member | Force Type | Typical Magnitude | Material |
|---|---|---|---|
| Deck girder | Bending + shear | 50 – 200 kN·m | Steel / concrete |
| Hangers | Tension | 500 – 2,000 kN each | Wire rope |
| Main cable | Tension | **500,000 – 2,000,000 kN** | Parallel wire strand |
| Tower | Compression | 200,000 – 800,000 kN | Steel or concrete |
| Anchor block | Tension pull-out | 300,000 – 1,500,000 kN | Concrete + rock |

**Why the cable tension is so enormous:** it carries the *entire bridge* in one continuous element. The Golden Gate's main cables each contain **27,572 wires** — laid end-to-end they would circle the Earth **3.5 times**.

> The elegance of suspension: a bridge that weighs hundreds of thousands of tonnes hangs from wires thinner than your wrist, because tension is the most efficient force a material can carry.

title: The Tea Shop at the End of the Lane
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: path
title: The Tea Shop at the End of the Lane
category: Story
byline: a tiny choose-your-path, by Juniper
view: single
---
A four-minute story you steer yourself. Read a scene, then pick a door.
Every choice is a [[z=1|cross-plane link]] that walks you to another plane.
There is no wrong path, only different cups of tea.

@plane z=0 label="The Lane"
# The Lane

Rain has just stopped. The lane smells of wet stone and woodsmoke, and at the
very end a small shop glows amber through fogged glass. A hand-painted sign
sways: **open, probably**.

You walk up. There is a brass **knocker** shaped like a sleeping fox, and a
door already left a finger's width **ajar**.

- Lift the [[z=1|fox knocker]]
- Slip through the [[z=2|open door]]

@plane z=1 label="You Knock"
# You lift the knocker

It is warm, which knockers should not be. Before you can rap it twice the door
opens itself, and a voice like steam says, *"Punctual. I like that."*

Inside: shelves to the ceiling, every jar labeled in a language of smells.

- Ask for [[z=3|"whatever I need"]]
- Ask for [[z=4|"something brave"]]

@plane z=2 label="You Enter"
# You slip inside

No one is here, but a single cup waits on the counter, still steaming, with a
note: *"For the curious. Sit anywhere."*

You sit. The chair sighs happily. The shop, you realize, has been **waiting**.

- Drink before [[z=3|anyone notices]]
- Wait, politely, for [[z=4|the owner]]

@plane z=3 label="What You Need"
# "Whatever I need"

The shopkeeper, who is mostly silhouette and entirely kind, considers you for
a long moment, then pours something pale gold.

*"This one is honest,"* they warn. *"Drink slowly."*

- [[z=5|Drink it]]

@plane z=4 label="Something Brave"
# "Something brave"

A grin in the dark. *"Now we are talking."* They reach to the highest shelf,
to a jar that hums, and measure three careful drops into roaring hot water.

*"Bravery is mostly just the first sip,"* they say.

- [[z=5|Take the first sip]]

@plane z=5 label="The good cup"
# The cup you needed

It tastes like the afternoon you forgot you missed: sunlight on a kitchen
table, a book you never finished, someone humming in the next room.

You leave a coin you didn't know you had. The fox knocker winks.

> Begin again from [[z=0|the lane]]?

title: ASCII Walk Cycle
src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: frame
title: ASCII Walk Cycle
fps: 8
loop: true
direction: rightward
artist: CorvidLabs
---
A four-frame walking loop drawn in monospace ASCII. Play the frames in order
(0, 1, 2, 3) and then jump back to [[z=0|frame 0]] to repeat. The figure keeps
its head and torso fixed while the legs and arms swing through a contact, a
recoil, a passing pose, and a high point, the four poses every classic walk
cycle is built from.

Read each plane as a single drawn cel. Render at 8 fps for a natural stroll;
drop to 4 fps for a slow, heavy trudge.

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="Contact"
# Frame 0 - Contact

The lead foot has just landed and the trailing foot is still pushing off. This
is the widest stride of the cycle and the pose viewers read as "a step."

```
  O
 /|\
 / \
/   \
```

- Lead leg: forward and straight, heel striking down
- Trail leg: extended back, toe still grounded
- Arms: opposed to legs (the wide `/|\` swing)

Next pose absorbs the impact: see [[z=1|frame 1, Recoil]].

@plane z=1 x=2 y=1 label="Recoil"
# Frame 1 - Recoil

The body sinks to its lowest point as the front leg takes the full weight. Knees
bend, the torso dips by one row, and momentum is briefly soaked up before the
push forward.

```
  O
 /|\
  |
 / \
```

- Lead leg: bent, bearing weight directly under the hips
- Trail leg: lifting, beginning to swing through
- Body height: lowest in the cycle (the dip sells the weight)

Coming up out of the dip leads to [[z=2|frame 2, Passing]].

@plane z=2 x=4 y=0 label="Passing"
# Frame 2 - Passing

The trailing leg passes underneath the body and the two legs cross. The torso is
balanced over a single support leg, rising back toward neutral height. Arms are
nearly straight down at the midpoint of their swing.

```
  O
 /|\
  |
  |
```

- Support leg: vertical, directly under the head
- Free leg: passing forward, knee high
- Arms: hanging near center, about to reverse direction

This is the balance point between [[z=1|the recoil]] and the next high step,
[[z=3|frame 3, High Point]].

@plane z=3 x=6 y=-1 label="High Point"
# Frame 3 - High Point

The body lifts to its highest point as the passing leg reaches forward and the
support leg straightens onto its toe. One more frame and the reaching foot will
land, returning the figure to the contact pose.

```
  O
 /|\
 / \
 \
```

- Reaching leg: swinging forward, heel about to drop
- Support leg: pushing up onto the toe (tallest pose)
- Arms: opposed swing widening again, mirroring frame 0

The reaching foot lands and the loop restarts at [[z=0|frame 0, Contact]].

Welcome to **Whimsy Hollow**, a park of three worlds stacked one atop the other — peel back each layer to reveal rides, services, and the hidden water realm beneath it all.

@plane z=0 label="Terrain & Paths"

# Terrain — The Land Itself

The bones of the park: paths, water, and greenery.

```
. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
```

- `W` Central Fountain — meet-up landmark
- `B` Briar Bridge — connects North and South zones
- `T` Woodland buffer — noise dampening, picnic clearings

> **Fast Pass lanes** available at 🎢 Thunderpine Run and 🎢 Serpent's Spine. Purchase at [[z=2|any Food & Services kiosk]].

**Accessibility:** All food venues have step-free access. Quiet room located adjacent to 🍦 Frostpine Creamery (north side).

@plane z=3 label="Hidden: Waterways"

# The Waterways — What Lies Beneath

The park's secret circulatory system: flume channels, misting pipes, and the underground river.

```

~ . . ~ ~ ~ ~ ~ ~ ~ ~ ~ . . . . . . . ~
~ . . . ~ ~ ~ ~ ~ ~ ~ . . . . . . . . ~
~ . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . ~
~ ~ ~ ~ ~ ~ ~ ~ ~ ~ ~ ~ ~ ~ ~ ~ ~ ~ ~ ~
```

The **Shimmer Lake** (see [[z=0|Terrain layer]]) feeds three hidden channels:

1. **The Flume Feed** — pressurized pipe supplying the park's two log-flume ride holding pools (not shown on public map — staff only)
2. **The Mist Ring** — low-pressure loop running beneath the main promenade; surface vents create cooling mist on hot days
3. **The Lost River** — a natural underground stream predating the park, preserved and protected; viewable through a glass floor panel inside the Hollow Tent attraction ([[z=1|Rides layer]])

> *"The water remembers the hollow long before the rides arrived."*
> — Park historian Maren Holloway, 1987 dedication plaque
